key_constructs.reasoning: The class centralizes configuration passed from the CLI (model, env_type, image, max_iterations, timeouts). Modifying its initialization or methods affects how the client is resolved, how environment resources are created/cleaned, and how messages are recorded and converted to Hermes format.

key_constructs.name: _create_env
key_constructs.purpose: Instantiates the environment for a task via create_environment and assigns to self.env.
key_constructs.reasoning: Creates the persistent workspace used for subsequent tool calls in the same task; callers assume a filesystem persistence between calls in the same task.

key_constructs.name: _cleanup_env
key_constructs.purpose: Cleans up or stops the environment (calls .cleanup() or .stop() if present).
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures containers or cloud resources are released. Editing should preserve the dual-method cleanup semantics to avoid leaking resources in different environment implementations.

key_constructs.name: _execute_command
key_constructs.purpose: Executes a provided bash command in the active environment and returns a dict with output, exit_code, and error.
key_constructs.reasoning: This bridges tool invocation and environment execution. It wraps exceptions into a standardized result shape; tests and consumers expect keys 'output', 'exit_code', 'error'.

key_constructs.name: _format_tools_for_system_message
key_constructs.purpose: Formats self.tools into a JSON string inserted into the system prompt describing available tools.
key_constructs.reasoning: The system prompt contains tool metadata; changes here affect what the LLM sees about tools and therefore how it composes tool calls.

key_constructs.name: _convert_to_hermes_format
key_constructs.purpose: Transforms the internal message list into Hermes trajectory entries (system/human/gpt/tool pairs with XML-like wrappers).
key_constructs.reasoning: This method is authoritative for output shape compatibility with batch_runner.py and the compression pipeline. Its exact tags (<tool_call>, <tool_response>, ) and field mapping are relied upon by downstream tooling.

key_constructs.name: run_task
key_constructs.purpose: Main loop for executing a single prompt: repeatedly call the LLM, execute tool calls, collect responses, and detect final completion.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function is the runtime core — it drives API calls, parses returned assistant messages (assistant_message.tool_calls), executes commands, appends tool results to the message history, and uses _convert_to_hermes_format to generate the trajectory. Any edits must preserve iteration limits, response parsing expectations, and final-output detection semantics.

key_behaviors: creates an execution environment per task
calls the configured LLM iteratively to obtain tool calls
executes terminal commands returned as tool calls
collects and converts conversation + tool call/response into Hermes trajectories

pitfalls.mistake: Assume the LLM client returns the exact same object shape across providers (choices[0].message.tool_calls, message.content, etc.).
pitfalls.consequence: AttributeErrors or incorrect parsing when different provider clients expose different response structures.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that different client adapters may vary; do not change parsing without checking provider adapters (agent.auxiliary_client) and the client library used.

pitfalls.mistake: Remove or rename the 'MINI_SWE_AGENT_FINAL_OUTPUT' marker detection semantics or the <tool_call>/<tool_response> tags in _convert_to_hermes_format.
pitfalls.consequence: Downstream processes (batch_runner.py, trajectory_compressor.py) may not detect task completion or may mis-parse tool responses, breaking batch processing compatibility.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the marker strings and tag conventions unless downstream consumers are updated as well.

pitfalls.mistake: Change the standardized result dict keys returned by _execute_command (output, exit_code, error).
pitfalls.consequence: Consumers in run_task expect those keys to form tool response content; mismatches will break JSON serialization and final trajectory content.
pitfalls.prevention: When refactoring, maintain the exact keys or update all call sites and serialization logic in the same commit.

pitfalls.mistake: Modify logging.basicConfig in __init__ without considering global logging effects.
pitfalls.consequence: Altering global logging configuration may change logging behavior across imported modules or tests.
pitfalls.prevention: If changing logging, ensure it doesn't inadvertently reconfigure logging for other modules or the test harness.

pitfalls.mistake: Assume tools.environments.* implementations are always present or have the same cleanup API.
pitfalls.consequence: ImportErrors or resource leaks if environment modules are not available or differ.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep lazy imports and the defensive cleanup code (checks for .cleanup and .stop) intact.

key_sections: _convert_to_hermes_format: conversion rules for Hermes trajectory output (tags and mapping)
TERMINAL_TOOL_DEFINITION: tool contract the model will use
create_environment: how environments are instantiated and which modules are imported
skip_unless_needed: module docstring/header comments
print statements and simple logging configuration

tests.example_command: No unit tests provided for this file; run higher-level integration scenarios manually.

related_files.relationship: Environment implementation used by create_environment('local').
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to LocalEnvironment constructor, execute(), cleanup()/stop(), or return dict shape must be reflected here.

related_files.relationship: Environment implementation used by create_environment('docker').
related_files.reason_to_check: Docker environment lifecycle and execute() semantics (return shape, argument names) should match expectations in this runner.

related_files.relationship: Environment implementation used by create_environment('modal').
related_files.reason_to_check: Modal cloud environment differences (async, streaming, credentials) affect how commands are executed and cleaned up.

related_files.relationship: Provider/client resolution helper used when api_key/base_url are not provided.
related_files.reason_to_check: If resolve_provider_client's return shape changes (client object interface), this runner's client usage must be updated.

related_files.relationship: Target consumer/compatible format for produced Hermes trajectories mentioned in the header.
related_files.reason_to_check: When editing the output format, verify compatibility with batch_runner.py and compression pipeline.

related_files.relationship: Downstream tool that compresses trajectories in the same Hermes format.
related_files.reason_to_check: If the trajectory tags or JSON shape change, update the compressor to match.

tests_to_run: No dedicated unit tests supplied for this module. For local verification, run small manual scenarios:
data_constants_to_check: LLM provider env vars: OPENROUTER_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY

rl_cli.py.short_description: Dedicated CLI entrypoint to run RL training workflows with extended timeouts and RL tools.
rl_cli.py.description:
This file provides a specialized command-line interface for reinforcement-learning-focused workflows that wrap the main agent runtime. It exists to: (1) provide RL-oriented defaults (longer max iterations, an RL-focused ephemeral system prompt, and default RL toolsets), (2) bootstrap environment variables from project and user .env files, (3) verify presence of the tinker-atropos submodule and RL-related API keys, and (4) create and invoke an AIAgent configured for RL tasks either in single-task or interactive mode. The script is intended for long-running training orchestration and interactive exploration of RL environments in the tinker-atropos submodule.

Design-wise the file is a thin orchestrator: it loads configuration from ~/.hermes/config.yaml (via load_hermes_config), performs readiness checks (check_requirements, check_tinker_atropos), provides several convenience actions (list_environments_sync, check_server), and then instantiates run_agent.AIAgent with RL-specific config such as RL_SYSTEM_PROMPT, RL_TOOLSETS, and extended RL_MAX_ITERATIONS. It uses the fire library to expose main() as the CLI entry point and supports both an interactive REPL-style loop and single task execution. The file delegates heavy behavior (model calls, tool execution, RL tool implementations) to external modules: run_agent (for AIAgent) and tools/rl_training_tool (for RL-specific helpers like get_missing_keys, rl_list_environments, rl_list_runs).

When editing, treat this file as an integration-level CLI wrapper: changes here affect how the agent is constructed, what environment checks run, what env files are loaded, and how user-facing flows (interactive loop, list/check commands) behave. The script performs synchronous blocking calls (agent.run_conversation) and uses asyncio.run to wrap async helper functions from tools.rl_training_tool. It prints user-facing guidance and status messages, and relies on several external modules and files (hermes_constants, hermes_cli.env_loader, run_agent, tools/rl_training_tool, and the optional tinker-atropos submodule).

key_constructs.name: DEFAULT_MODEL
key_constructs.purpose: Default model string used when no model is provided or configured.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to choose the model for RL tasks if the user or ~/.hermes/config.yaml doesn't specify one; changing it changes the default provider used for long-running RL workflows.

key_constructs.name: DEFAULT_BASE_URL
key_constructs.purpose: Default base URL for API calls (sourced from hermes_constants.OPENROUTER_BASE_URL).
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the base endpoint used when no base_url is passed; edits must respect that it mirrors hermes_constants.

key_constructs.name: load_hermes_config
key_constructs.purpose: Read ~/.hermes/config.yaml and return effective config (model and base_url).
key_constructs.reasoning: This function encapsulates how user configuration is loaded and prioritized; callers in main rely on its dictionary shape and keys ('model', 'base_url').

key_constructs.name: RL_MAX_ITERATIONS
key_constructs.purpose: Default iteration limit for RL workflows (200).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to configure AIAgent.max_iterations for RL; impacts how long agents will loop before stopping.

key_constructs.name: RL_SYSTEM_PROMPT
key_constructs.purpose: Ephemeral system prompt tailored for RL post-training engineering guidance.
key_constructs.reasoning: Passed to the agent to bias behavior for RL tasks and tool usage; it's multi-line and user-visible when creating the agent.

key_constructs.name: RL_TOOLSETS
key_constructs.purpose: List of toolsets to enable by default for RL workflows.
key_constructs.reasoning: Controls which toolset names are passed to the agent on instantiation; must align with tool names implemented elsewhere (e.g., 'rl', 'terminal', 'web').

key_constructs.name: check_requirements
key_constructs.purpose: Verify required environment variables and RL API keys are present.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used before creating the agent; returns False and prints messages when keys are missing; callers may exit on False.

key_constructs.name: check_tinker_atropos
key_constructs.purpose: Verify the tinker-atropos submodule and its environments directory exist.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by the --check-server flow and to help users debug submodule setup; returns (bool, info_or_message) so callers can decide how to present results.

key_constructs.name: list_environments_sync
key_constructs.purpose: Synchronous wrapper that calls tools.rl_training_tool.rl_list_environments (async) and returns the parsed JSON result.
key_constructs.reasoning: Bridges async RL helpers to this synchronous CLI script; it uses asyncio.run and expects rl_list_environments to return a JSON string.

key_constructs.name: main
key_constructs.purpose: CLI entry point exposed via fire.Fire; handles parsing flags, running environment checks, listing environments, and creating/invoking the RL-configured AIAgent.
key_constructs.reasoning: All user flows are implemented here: --check-server, --list-environments, interactive loop, and single-task execution. Understanding main() is essential to change CLI behavior or agent configuration.

key_behaviors: loads user and project environment variables before running
validates presence of required API keys for RL tools
lists available tinker-atropos RL environments
creates and runs an AIAgent for single or interactive RL tasks

pitfalls.mistake: Calling or assuming get_hermes_home() is defined before importing it.
pitfalls.consequence: NameError/ImportError at module import time (the file calls get_hermes_home() before importing it from hermes_constants).
pitfalls.prevention: When editing, be aware of import and execution order at top-level; avoid moving or removing imports that supply symbols used during module import.

pitfalls.mistake: Treating the CLI as unit-testable without stubbing heavy imports (run_agent, tools.rl_training_tool).
pitfalls.consequence: Running tests that import this module may trigger heavy side effects or require unavailable API keys and submodules (tinker-atropos), causing failures or long delays.
pitfalls.prevention: Prefer running the CLI script in isolation for manual testing and mock/stub run_agent or tools.* functions when writing automated tests.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying RL_TOOLSETS or RL_SYSTEM_PROMPT without checking corresponding tool implementations.
pitfalls.consequence: Agent may be configured to use toolsets that don't exist or mismatch tool names, leading to runtime errors when the agent attempts tool invocation.
pitfalls.prevention: Cross-check toolset names with the tools package and ensure AIAgent accepts the given config keys.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming agent.run_conversation is non-blocking or async.
pitfalls.consequence: Interactive loop or single-task flows could block unexpectedly; changes that introduce new event loops or async/await semantics may conflict.
pitfalls.prevention: Confirm the AIAgent API (synchronous vs async) before changing how it is called; maintain the synchronous call pattern used here unless coordinating with run_agent.

key_sections: Top-level env loading: code that calls get_hermes_home and load_hermes_dotenv (module import/bootstrapping)
load_hermes_config: how ~/.hermes/config.yaml is read and prioritized
check_requirements/check_tinker_atropos: readiness checks used before agent creation
main: CLI flags and flows (check-server, list-environments, interactive, single-task)
skip_unless_needed: RL_SYSTEM_PROMPT content (very large static string) if only editing CLI logic
print/emoji user-facing messages unless modifying user-visible text

related_files.relationship: Provides AIAgent class that this CLI instantiates and uses to run the conversation/task.
related_files.reason_to_check: If AIAgent's constructor signature or run_conversation semantics change, update main() to match.

related_files.relationship: Supplies RL helper functions referenced here (get_missing_keys, rl_list_environments, rl_list_runs).
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to these helper function signatures or return formats (e.g., JSON strings vs objects) require updates to list_environments_sync and the interactive status flow.

related_files.relationship: Used to load .env files before main execution; return value is printed and influences environment availability.
related_files.reason_to_check: If environment loading behavior changes (paths, precedence), CLI behavior and key checks will be affected.

related_files.relationship: Provides get_hermes_home and OPENROUTER_BASE_URL consumed by this file.
related_files.reason_to_check: This file is used at module import-time—alterations to get_hermes_home or constant names affect top-level initialization in rl_cli.py.

related_files.relationship: Optional submodule containing RL environments; listing and training operate on files here.
related_files.reason_to_check: If the submodule structure changes (paths, filenames), the environment listing and path checks will need to be adapted.

tests_to_run: Run the repository test suite (pytest) but be cautious: importing this module in tests may require stubbing heavy dependencies.
Manual smoke runs: python rl_cli.py --check-server and python rl_cli.py --list-environments (ensure tinker-atropos present or handle fallback).

utils.py.short_description: Shared helpers for truthy env coercion and atomic JSON/YAML file writes.
utils.py.description:
This module centralizes a small set of utility behaviours used across the codebase: coercing various representations of truthy values, checking environment variables for truthiness, and performing atomic writes for JSON and YAML files. The atomic write helpers are designed to avoid partially-written configuration or state files by writing to a temporary file in the destination directory, fsync'ing the file descriptor, and then atomically replacing the target path using os.replace. The utilities ensure parent directories exist and use UTF-8 encoding.

The truthiness helpers provide a consistent interpretation of boolean-like inputs across the project (TRUTHY_STRINGS defines the recognized text values). env_var_enabled() is a thin wrapper around is_truthy_value() that reads the environment and returns a boolean. The atomic JSON and YAML writers explicitly use tempfile.mkstemp with the target directory to avoid cross-filesystem rename problems and take care to cleanup temporary files even when process-level signals (KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit) occur; this is done via catching BaseException and re-raising after attempted cleanup.

These functions exist to be low-level primitives other subsystems rely on when persisting state, configuration, or when reading environment flags (e.g., enabling debug, features, or gateway adapters). Consumers expect atomic behavior (no corrupted or partial files), consistent YAML/JSON formatting options, and a stable truthiness policy for environment-driven booleans.

key_constructs.name: TRUTHY_STRINGS
key_constructs.purpose: Defines the set of string values treated as truthy when coercing strings to booleans.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the canonical textual truth values so all callers share the same interpretation; editing this constant changes the behaviour globally for is_truthy_value/env_var_enabled.

key_constructs.name: is_truthy_value
key_constructs.purpose: Coerces different input types (None, bool, str, others) into a boolean according to project policy.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used wherever values may be strings, booleans, or None (for example parsing config or env values). Callers expect None to map to a default and strings to be case-insensitively checked against TRUTHY_STRINGS.

key_constructs.name: env_var_enabled
key_constructs.purpose: Reads an environment variable and returns True if it represents a truthy value.
key_constructs.reasoning: Thin convenience wrapper that centralizes environment reading + truthiness check; callers assume default behavior of returning False when the env is unset or not truthy.

key_constructs.name: atomic_json_write
key_constructs.purpose: Atomically write JSON-serializable data to a path by writing into a temp file, fsync'ing and replacing the target.
key_constructs.reasoning: Designed to prevent partially-written JSON files. The implementation intentionally uses mkstemp in the same directory, os.fdopen, f.flush/os.fsync, and os.replace, and it catches BaseException to ensure temporary file cleanup on interrupts.

key_constructs.name: atomic_yaml_write
key_constructs.purpose: Atomically write YAML data (and optional extra content) to a path using the same atomic pattern as for JSON.
key_constructs.reasoning: Preserves the same atomic semantics for YAML files; supports appending extra_content (e.g., comments) and controls YAML flow style / key sorting.

key_behaviors: coerces arbitrary inputs to boolean truthiness
reads environment variables and maps to booleans
creates parent directories for target files
writes JSON/YAML files atomically to avoid partial writes

pitfalls.mistake: Moving temporary file creation to a different directory (not the target's parent).
pitfalls.consequence: os.replace may fail or not be atomic across filesystems; atomic guarantees would be lost and partial files may remain.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure mkstemp is called with dir=str(path.parent) as implemented; keep that behaviour when editing.

pitfalls.mistake: Catching only Exception instead of BaseException or removing the cleanup block.
pitfalls.consequence: KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit during writes could leave orphaned temp files on disk and not perform expected cleanup before process exits.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing BaseException catch + re-raise pattern so cleanup runs on process-level interruptions.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing f.flush() or os.fsync() calls.
pitfalls.consequence: Data may remain in user-space buffers and not be durable on disk when os.replace runs, which risks corruption if the process or system crashes.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep explicit flush and os.fsync calls prior to os.replace to preserve atomic durability guarantees.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing encoding from 'utf-8' or writing binary mode unintentionally.
pitfalls.consequence: Non-UTF-8 data may be written incorrectly; downstream readers expect UTF-8 JSON/YAML text.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain 'w' mode with encoding='utf-8' as in current implementation.

key_sections: is_truthy_value: core truthiness coercion used by env_var_enabled and other callers
atomic_json_write: atomic JSON write pattern (mkstemp, fdopen, json.dump, f.flush, os.fsync, os.replace)
atomic_yaml_write: same atomic write pattern for YAML with optional extra_content
skip_unless_needed: module imports and constants (TRUTHY_STRINGS) if you only need file-write behaviour

related_files.relationship: Consumes atomic file writes for persisting memories/state; may rely on atomicity when updating disk-backed stores.
related_files.reason_to_check: If persistence format or write semantics change (indentation, ensure_ascii, YAML extra content), memory_manager behavior or migrations may need adjustments.

related_files.relationship: Configuration loader/writer uses JSON/YAML utilities to persist user configuration and expects parent directory creation and atomic replacement.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to atomic write signatures or default parameters (indent, sort_keys) affect how CLI writes configs; verify consumers' expectations.

related_files.relationship: Gateway components may persist runtime state or credentials; they depend on reliable atomic writes to avoid corrupted state.
related_files.reason_to_check: If write behaviour is modified (e.g., removal of fsync), gateway persistence-related bugs could surface.

data_constants_to_check: encoding and mkstemp dir usage (must be same dir as target)

setup-hermes.sh.short_description: Interactive bootstrap script to provision Python 3.11, install deps, and wire the hermes CLI for local development
setup-hermes.sh.description:
This Bash script exists to provide a single-step developer onboarding experience for the Hermes Agent repository. It locates or installs the 'uv' tool (astral.sh/uv), uses uv to provision Python 3.11, creates a project-local virtual environment (venv), installs the project's dependencies (preferring a hash-verified uv.lock sync when present with a pip fallback), attempts to install optional submodules, seeds bundled skills into the user HERMES_HOME skills directory, ensures ~/.local/bin is on PATH (and symlinks the hermes CLI from the venv there), and offers to run the hermes setup wizard at the end.

The script is intentionally interactive: it prompts the user for ripgrep installation and whether to run the setup wizard. It also writes to the user's shell config (e.g., ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc) to add ~/.local/bin to PATH if needed. The design centralizes common onboarding steps so contributors can get a reproducible dev environment quickly while still permitting fallbacks (pip install when uv.lock fails, copying skills if the Python-based sync fails).

Because the script makes environment and filesystem changes (creates/removes venv directory, modifies shell rc files, symlinks into ~/.local/bin, copies into HERMES_HOME), edits to it must respect its side effects and idempotence semantics. Several operational decisions are encoded as explicit flows: uv discovery & installation, uv.lock prioritized installs with a pip fallback, submodule detection for optional RL backend, and a best-effort skills sync that tolerates missing dependencies. The script is written for Bash (relies on BASH_SOURCE, [[ ... ]], read -p) and uses set -e to fail fast on unexpected errors.

key_constructs.name: SCRIPT_DIR
key_constructs.purpose: Repository root directory where the script lives; used as the working directory for venv creation, pip installs, skills sync, and locating files like .env.example
key_constructs.reasoning: Most file operations are relative to the repo root; preserving SCRIPT_DIR behavior ensures paths (venv, tools/skills_sync.py, skills/) continue to resolve correctly when the script is run from anywhere.

key_constructs.name: PYTHON_VERSION
key_constructs.purpose: Pinned Python version string ('3.11') used for uv python provisioning and venv creation
key_constructs.reasoning: This value drives which Python is requested from uv and is central to reproducible dev environments; changing it affects uv python find/install and venv interpreter selection.

key_constructs.name: UV_CMD
key_constructs.purpose: Command or path used to invoke the 'uv' tool; discovered or installed during the script's run
key_constructs.reasoning: uv is used for multiple operations (python find/install, creating venv, pip). The script sets UV_CMD early and reuses it; edits must preserve how UV_CMD is located and used.

key_constructs.name: VIRTUAL_ENV
key_constructs.purpose: Environment variable exported to tell uv to install into the created venv (points at SCRIPT_DIR/venv)
key_constructs.reasoning: uv uses VIRTUAL_ENV to know target venv; preserving this export keeps subsequent uv sync/pip operations targeting the repo-local venv without requiring manual activation.

key_constructs.name: HERMES_BIN
key_constructs.purpose: Resolved path to the hermes console script inside the created venv (venv/bin/hermes) used to create a symlink in ~/.local/bin
key_constructs.reasoning: The symlink operation expects a hermes console script at this path; changes that alter packaging or entry points must be coordinated with this reference.

key_constructs.name: HERMES_SKILLS_DIR
key_constructs.purpose: Target directory for syncing bundled skills; resolved from HERMES_HOME environment variable or defaults to ~/.hermes/skills
key_constructs.reasoning: Skill synchronization writes into the user's persistent data directory; edits must keep this semantics so skills land in the same runtime location the application expects.

key_behaviors: discovers or installs the uv tool
provisions Python 3.11 via uv and creates a repo-local venv
installs dependencies preferring uv.lock with pip fallback
symlinks the hermes console script into ~/.local/bin and updates shell config
creates .env from .env.example and syncs bundled skills

pitfalls.mistake: Running the script non-interactively in CI without addressing interactive prompts (read -p) or network requirements
pitfalls.consequence: Script can block waiting for user input or fail on curl/install steps, causing CI runs or automation to hang or error
pitfalls.prevention: When automating, provide non-interactive flags or pre-seed environment (e.g., set answers or skip ripgrep prompt) and ensure network access for curl-based installers

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or altering the 'venv' path assumption (the script rm -rf venv and recreates it)
pitfalls.consequence: Unintended deletion of an important venv if the repo layout differs or a user has existing important state in that path
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the explicit venv name and avoid changing it without coordinating downstream references like HERMES_BIN and VIRTUAL_ENV

pitfalls.mistake: Changing how PATH is detected/modified or the selected shell config file logic
pitfalls.consequence: Users may not get ~/.local/bin on PATH; hermes symlink will not be found on new shells, producing confusion
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the idempotent logic that checks PATH and shell rc contents before appending the export line

pitfalls.mistake: Replacing uv.lock synchronous install logic without keeping a robust fallback
pitfalls.consequence: Dependency installation can fail on systems lacking uv support or with stale lockfiles, preventing dev setup
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the current two-step flow: try hashed uv.lock sync, then fall back to pip install -e with extras

pitfalls.mistake: Overwriting existing user shell config files or blindly appending duplicate PATH export lines
pitfalls.consequence: Pollutes user's shell configs and may introduce duplicate PATH modifications or formatting issues
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the checks that avoid duplicating .local/bin entries and append the export only when missing

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming tools/skills_sync.py will always succeed (and removing the copy fallback)
pitfalls.consequence: If the sync script fails due to missing deps, skills might not be provisioned into HERMES_HOME and features relying on bundled skills will be missing
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the two-stage approach: try python sync script, and if it fails, fall back to a conservative copy from skills/ if present

key_sections: uv discovery/install: determines or installs UV_CMD and validates availability
Python provisioning & venv creation: uses uv to find/install Python and create the venv
Dependencies install: uv.lock sync with pip fallback and the exact pip install -e invocation
Shell PATH and symlink block: computes HERMES_BIN, symlinks into ~/.local/bin and edits shell rc files
Skills sync and .env creation: seeds bundled skills and copies .env.example
skip_unless_needed: ripgrep interactive installation branch (only relevant when adding optional tool support)
submodule tinker-atropos section if the contributor does not use RL tools

tests.example_command: ./setup-hermes.sh  # run in a controlled dev environment (interactive)

related_files.relationship: Used as template to create .env when one is missing
related_files.reason_to_check: If .env.example keys or format change, the setup script's .env creation semantics (copy) remain valid but you should confirm expected variables are present

related_files.relationship: Preferred lockfile for uv sync-based dependency installation
related_files.reason_to_check: If uv.lock is added, removed, or updated, the install branch in this script behaves differently; verify success/fallback behavior

related_files.relationship: Primary mechanism the script invokes to populate HERMES_HOME skills
related_files.reason_to_check: If the sync script's CLI or runtime requirements change, the call in setup-hermes.sh must remain compatible or fallback copy logic will run

related_files.relationship: Fallback source of bundled skills when the sync script cannot run
related_files.reason_to_check: If bundled skill layout or expectations change, ensure the simple copy fallback still produces a valid skills directory layout

related_files.relationship: Defines installable package metadata and extras referenced by pip install -e ".[all]"
related_files.reason_to_check: If packaging or extras change, the pip install -e invocation might need to be adjusted to ensure required extras are installed

related_files.relationship: Entry point invoked by the script at the end via 'python -m hermes_cli.main setup' to run the interactive setup wizard
related_files.reason_to_check: If the CLI module path or setup subcommand changes, the end-of-script invocation must be kept in sync

tests_to_run: Manual verification: run ./setup-hermes.sh in a clean VM or container with network access
Validate hermes binary: ensure ~/.local/bin/hermes symlink points to venv/bin/hermes and is executable
Validate PATH: open a new shell or source the modified shell config file to confirm ~/.local/bin is on PATH
Validate venv contents: confirm packages installed (pip list) inside venv and that hermes_cli module is importable
data_constants_to_check: PYTHON_VERSION (must reflect supported/minimum Python for project)
Paths that depend on SCRIPT_DIR: venv, tools/skills_sync.py, skills/

agent/models_dev.py.short_description: Integration with the community models.dev registry: fetch, cache, and expose provider/model metadata and capability helpers.
agent/models_dev.py.description:
This module provides a single authoritative integration point for reading and querying the models.dev registry (https://models.dev/api.json). It exists to centralize parsing, caching, and interpretation of provider and model metadata so other parts of Hermes (model routing, UI model lists, capability checks) never parse the raw JSON directly. The module implements an offline-first resolution order (bundled snapshot / disk cache / network fetch) and an in-memory TTL cache to avoid repeated network calls.

Key responsibilities include: fetching and caching the registry (fetch_models_dev), mapping Hermes provider IDs to models.dev provider IDs (PROVIDER_TO_MODELS_DEV and _get_reverse_mapping), exposing typed dataclasses (ModelInfo, ProviderInfo, ModelCapabilities) that capture commonly used fields, and providing utility lookup/search functions (lookup_models_dev_context, get_model_capabilities, list_provider_models, list_agentic_models, search_models_dev). The module also implements heuristics (a regex) to filter out non-agentic/noise models for UI selection and tooling. Disk writes use the atomic_json_write utility to avoid producing corrupt cache files.

Design decisions visible in the code: use of small dataclasses to present typed model/provider metadata to callers; a multi-tier cache (in-memory TTL + disk) with network fallback and debug-logged errors (so callers receive an empty dict instead of an exception on failures); case-insensitive model lookups to tolerate provider/model naming differences; and conservative default capability values when models.dev entries lack limits (e.g., default huge context_window / max_output_tokens). The module mutates module-level globals for caches and reverse mappings and intentionally favors resilience to network failure (fallbacks to disk, non-fatal logs) so the rest of the agent can operate even without connectivity.

key_constructs.name: ModelInfo
key_constructs.purpose: Dataclass representing rich metadata for a single model entry (capabilities, modalities, limits, cost, metadata).
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a stable, typed structure for other parts of the system to consume models.dev data without parsing raw dicts; includes convenience methods (format_cost, supports_vision, etc.) used by UIs or diagnostics.

key_constructs.name: ProviderInfo
key_constructs.purpose: Dataclass representing metadata for a provider (id, display name, env vars, API URL, docs).
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates provider-level metadata that the UI/credential manager or model routing logic may need; keeps provider fields consistent across callers.

key_constructs.name: ModelCapabilities
key_constructs.purpose: Condensed capability summary extracted from a models.dev model entry (tools, vision, reasoning, context/output limits, family).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used where only capability info is required (e.g., routing decisions, tool compatibility checks) without pulling full ModelInfo.

key_constructs.name: PROVIDER_TO_MODELS_DEV
key_constructs.purpose: Mapping from Hermes provider IDs to models.dev provider IDs.
key_constructs.reasoning: All lookups and provider-scoped searches rely on this mapping; it is the primary source of truth for translating Hermes provider names to the models.dev dataset.

key_constructs.name: _NOISE_PATTERNS
key_constructs.purpose: Regex pattern used to detect and exclude noisy/non-agentic model IDs (TTS, embeddings, image-only previews etc.).
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes heuristics for filtering models that should not be offered for agentic tool use; used by list_agentic_models and search logic.

key_constructs.name: fetch_models_dev
key_constructs.purpose: Fetch the models.dev registry with in-memory TTL caching, disk cache fallback, and optional forced refresh.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the primary entrypoint for other modules to obtain the registry. It encapsulates network timeout, error handling, disk persistence, and the caching policy.

key_constructs.name: _save_disk_cache
key_constructs.purpose: Atomically write the registry snapshot to disk using atomic_json_write.
key_constructs.reasoning: Avoids partial/corrupt cache files on process interruption; file I/O errors are logged but do not raise to callers.

key_constructs.name: _load_disk_cache
key_constructs.purpose: Load a previously saved registry snapshot from disk, returning an empty dict on error.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides resilience to network failures by making a locally persisted snapshot available; callers get an empty dict if loading fails.

key_constructs.name: lookup_models_dev_context
key_constructs.purpose: Return the context window (tokens) for a given provider+model, supporting case-insensitive matching and filtering out zero-context entries.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by token estimation/prompt building to determine model context limits without consumers parsing models.dev themselves.

key_constructs.name: get_model_capabilities
key_constructs.purpose: Extract a ModelCapabilities instance for a provider+model using models.dev entries.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes how capability flags and limits are derived from raw models.dev data so routing/feature gating logic can call a single well-defined API.

key_constructs.name: list_agentic_models
key_constructs.purpose: Return model IDs suitable for agentic/tool usage by filtering tool_call=True and applying _NOISE_PATTERNS.
key_constructs.reasoning: Exposes a curated set of models for UI selection and internal agent tooling to avoid exposing TTS/embedding/image-only models by accident.

key_constructs.name: search_models_dev
key_constructs.purpose: Fuzzy search across the cached models.dev catalog, optionally restricted to a provider, returning matches with provider and entry data.
key_constructs.reasoning: Supports UI autocomplete and manual model discovery without callers having to implement fuzzy logic or iterate the registry.

key_constructs.name: _extract_context
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that pulls limit.context from a model entry and returns a positive int or None.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates validation logic for context values (filters out 0 or non-numeric) used by context lookups.

key_behaviors: fetches models.dev registry and caches it to memory and disk
maps Hermes provider IDs to models.dev provider IDs
extracts model capability and limit information for routing/selection
filters out non-agentic/noise models for UI/tooling

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming fetch_models_dev always returns fresh network data
pitfalls.consequence: Callers may receive stale data until the in-memory TTL expires; background refresh is not automatic unless force_refresh is used.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the TTL semantics when updating or force-refreshing; don't rely on immediate network-sourced changes without force_refresh.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying PROVIDER_TO_MODELS_DEV without updating all dependent logic or downstream mappings
pitfalls.consequence: Lookups may fail silently and functions like get_model_capabilities or list_provider_models will return empty results for mismatched providers.
pitfalls.prevention: When changing provider mappings, update reverse mappings and validate with unit tests that key providers resolve.

pitfalls.mistake: Treating module-level caches and globals as thread-safe
pitfalls.consequence: Concurrent access in multi-threaded contexts could read partially-updated globals or race updates, causing inconsistent behavior or lost cache writes.
pitfalls.prevention: Be aware that _models_dev_cache, _models_dev_cache_time and _MODELS_DEV_TO_PROVIDER are mutated globals; any changes that introduce concurrency must preserve atomicity or add synchronization.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming all models have valid numeric limits.context or limits.output
pitfalls.consequence: Code that expects integers may get None or 0 (which this module filters) leading to defaults being used; downstream token calculations could be overly permissive/incorrect.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that _extract_context returns None for zero/invalid values and that get_model_capabilities uses conservative defaults; tests should assert behavior for missing/zero limits.

pitfalls.mistake: Editing disk cache write logic without preserving atomic_json_write usage
pitfalls.consequence: Cache file corruption on interrupted writes, causing load failures and loss of offline snapshot.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep atomic write semantics (atomic_json_write) when modifying _save_disk_cache behavior.

key_sections: PROVIDER_TO_MODELS_DEV: primary mapping Hermes→models.dev used across lookups
fetch_models_dev: caching, network fetch, disk fallback, and TTL behavior
get_model_capabilities: how capability flags and limits are derived and defaulted
list_agentic_models and _NOISE_PATTERNS: filtering heuristics for agentic selection
skip_unless_needed: ModelInfo dataclass formatting helpers (format_cost/format_capabilities) if only capability lookup is needed
search_models_dev internals when only simple lookups are required

related_files.relationship: Provides atomic_json_write used for atomic disk cache writes.
related_files.reason_to_check: If changing cache write/read behavior, verify atomic_json_write semantics and error handling.

related_files.relationship: Provides get_hermes_home used to locate the disk cache path.
related_files.reason_to_check: If HERMES_HOME behavior changes, the cache file location and migrations must be revisited.

related_files.relationship: Likely consumer of ModelCapabilities and list_agentic_models for routing model/provider selection.
related_files.reason_to_check: If capability semantics or defaults change, routing logic should be validated.

tests_to_run: pytest -q  # run full suite (module-specific tests not present in repo snapshot)

insights.title: Add models.dev agentic model filtering
insights.problem: Live models.dev catalog includes models intended for TTS, embeddings, image-only, dated previews etc., which are not suitable for agent/tool usage.
insights.root_cause: Previously there was no filtering API to select only agentic/tool-call capable models.
insights.solution: Add list_agentic_models(provider) which fetches provider models and filters by entry['tool_call']=True and a noise regex that excludes TTS, embedding, live, dated previews, image-only, customtools.
insights.lesson_learned: When using a live registry, filter out non-agentic models and noisy entries; expose a helper that returns curated tool-capable models for UI selection. Tests should mock fetch_models_dev and assert filtering.

insights.title: Write models.dev disk cache atomically to avoid corruption
insights.problem: If process was killed mid-write while writing JSON cache, file could become corrupt and _load_disk_cache would silently return {} until next fetch.
insights.root_cause: Plain open()/json.dump() was used for cache writes, which is vulnerable to partial writes.
insights.solution: Use atomic_json_write() utility to write cache atomically (write to tmp and rename), preventing corrupt partial JSON files.
insights.lesson_learned: Any on-disk cache must be written atomically to avoid corruption and subtle cache-loss bugs; centralize atomic-writer usage.

toolset_distributions.py.short_description: Defines named distributions of toolsets and provides sampling/inspection utilities.
toolset_distributions.py.description:
This module centralizes definitions of many named "distributions" that map toolset names to integer probabilities (percentage-like values). It exists to provide a single authoritative place to express which toolsets should be available for synthetic data generation or batch prompt runs and to expose helpers for querying, validating, sampling, and printing those distributions. The DISTRIBUTIONS constant contains the domain data; the functions are small helpers used by other parts of the system or by data-generation tooling to pick toolsets per prompt.

Design decisions visible in the file: distributions are dictionaries keyed by distribution name and each distribution has a 'description' and a 'toolsets' mapping. Probabilities are modeled as integers in a 1..100 range and sampling treats each toolset as an independent Bernoulli trial — i.e., multiple toolsets can be selected simultaneously. The code defers toolset validity to an external dependency (validate_toolset from toolsets.py) so invalid toolset names are tolerated in definitions but warned about at sample-time and ignored. The sampling function also contains a deterministic fallback (if nothing is selected, pick the highest-probability toolset) to guarantee at least one toolset is returned.

This module also contains a small CLI/demo block under __main__ to print distributions and show sampling examples. Consumers: other scripts that need to decide available tools per generated prompt should call get_distribution / sample_toolsets_from_distribution, and tests validate the shape and value ranges of DISTRIBUTIONS. Because probability values and distribution names are used by generation pipelines, changes here are data/behavior sensitive and may affect downstream tooling, tests, and datasets.

key_constructs.name: DISTRIBUTIONS
key_constructs.purpose: Holds all named distributions; each value contains a description and a mapping of toolset -> probability.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the single source of truth for toolset availability used by sampling and introspection helpers; edits will directly change runtime behavior and test expectations.

key_constructs.name: get_distribution
key_constructs.purpose: Return the distribution dict for a given name or None if not found.
key_constructs.reasoning: Lightweight lookup used by other helpers and tests; consumers expect None for unknown names.

key_constructs.name: list_distributions
key_constructs.purpose: Return a shallow copy of the DISTRIBUTIONS mapping.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used for introspection and demos; callers may mutate the returned dict so identity semantics (copy vs same) matter for tests.

key_constructs.name: sample_toolsets_from_distribution
key_constructs.purpose: Sample a set/list of toolset names from a named distribution based on each toolset's probability.
key_constructs.reasoning: Core behavior: performs independent random inclusion per toolset, consults validate_toolset, warns about invalid toolsets, and enforces a fallback that ensures at least one toolset is returned if the random selection yields none.

key_constructs.name: validate_distribution
key_constructs.purpose: Return True if a distribution name exists in DISTRIBUTIONS.
key_constructs.reasoning: Simple membership check used by callers to verify available distributions; tests rely on exact membership semantics.

key_constructs.name: print_distribution_info
key_constructs.purpose: Pretty-print description and sorted toolsets of a distribution to stdout.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used in the module demo and useful for humans inspecting distributions; it formats percentages and orders toolsets by probability.

key_behaviors: provides named distributions for generation runs
samples toolsets from distributions using independent probability trials
validates distribution names and toolset names
ensures at least one toolset via deterministic fallback

pitfalls.mistake: Changing numeric probability values without updating tests or downstream expectations
pitfalls.consequence: Sampling output and datasets generated by pipelines will change; tests that assert probability ranges or presence may fail.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing DISTRIBUTIONS, run the test file and any generation pipelines that rely on specific distributions.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or renaming toolset keys referenced elsewhere or invalidating entries expected by validate_toolset
pitfalls.consequence: validate_toolset will flag toolsets as invalid and those toolsets will be skipped during sampling; the fallback may select a different toolset.
pitfalls.prevention: Verify changes against toolsets.validate_toolset and run integration flows that depend on those toolsets.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying sampling semantics (e.g., switching from independent Bernoulli trials to exclusive selection) without preserving fallback behavior
pitfalls.consequence: Tests that expect independent selection or the guarantee of at least one selected toolset will break; downstream selection assumptions will change.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect current sampling contract: independent inclusion per toolset, use validate_toolset check, and ensure fallback that picks the highest-probability valid toolset.

pitfalls.mistake: Relying on comments in the DISTRIBUTIONS block as the authoritative source of intent
pitfalls.consequence: Some inline comments are inconsistent with the numeric values (e.g., comment percentages not matching numbers); relying on them can lead to incorrect expectations.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat the numeric probability values as authoritative; if intent must change, update both numbers and any dependent tests.

key_sections: DISTRIBUTIONS: domain data; review to understand what distributions exist and their probabilities
sample_toolsets_from_distribution: main behavior for runtime selection and the fallback guarantee
validate_distribution / get_distribution: lookup helpers used by callers and tests
skip_unless_needed: __main__ demo block: human-oriented printing examples and not used by runtime consumers

relevant_snippets.description: Tests sampling from 'default' distribution (expect all 100% toolsets present) and related assertions.
Test that sampling 'reasoning' repeatedly always returns at least one toolset (the fallback behavior is exercised across multiple iterations).
Structural tests that assert every distribution has a description, toolsets mapping and probability values are in (0,100].

related_files.relationship: Provides validate_toolset used by sampling to ignore invalid toolset names; sampling behavior depends on its boolean result.
related_files.reason_to_check: If toolset names are added/removed or validate_toolset semantics change, DISTRIBUTIONS must be reconciled.

related_files.relationship: Unit tests that assert distribution shape, values, and sampling behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: When distribution values or sampling semantics change, update these tests accordingly.

data_constants_to_check: DISTRIBUTIONS: all distribution keys and probability integer values
toolsets.validate_toolset: ensure toolset names are valid

tests/test_toolset_distributions.py.short_description: Unit tests validating distribution definitions and sampling behavior.
tests/test_toolset_distributions.py.description:
This test module verifies the correctness of the toolset distributions data and the sampling/lookup helpers exposed by toolset_distributions.py. It exercises both structural properties (presence of 'description' and 'toolsets', probability ranges, non-empty descriptions) and runtime behaviors (lookup semantics, shallow-copy behavior from list_distributions, sampling outcomes, and the fallback guarantee). The tests are deterministic where possible (membership/structural checks) and probabilistic tests rely on repeated sampling to confirm fallback behavior rather than exact random outcomes.

The tests are organized into classes by concern: TestGetDistribution covers lookup behaviors and that the expected named distributions exist; TestListDistributions checks copy semantics and containment; TestValidateDistribution asserts membership logic; TestSampleToolsetsFromDistribution validates error handling for unknown names, deterministic semantics for 'default' (100% probabilities), basic expectations for 'minimal' and 'balanced', and the fallback guarantee for low-probability distributions; TestDistributionStructure enforces data hygiene (required keys, probability ranges, description lengths). Running these tests exercises the code paths that consult validate_toolset, use random.random, and use DISTRIBUTIONS directly.

key_constructs.name: TestGetDistribution
key_constructs.purpose: Group of tests that validate distribution lookup behavior (get_distribution) and existence of named distributions.
key_constructs.reasoning: Modifying get_distribution or distribution names will directly affect tests in this class.

key_constructs.name: TestListDistributions
key_constructs.purpose: Tests that list_distributions returns a copy and contains the same entries as DISTRIBUTIONS.
key_constructs.reasoning: Changes to list_distributions return semantics (identity/equality) will be caught here.

key_constructs.name: TestValidateDistribution
key_constructs.purpose: Tests membership checks for distribution names.
key_constructs.reasoning: validate_distribution must reflect exact membership semantics expected by these tests.

key_constructs.name: TestSampleToolsetsFromDistribution
key_constructs.purpose: Covers sampling semantics, error behavior, and fallback guarantee.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the most behavior-sensitive test class — changes to sampling, validate_toolset usage, or fallback will be detected here.

key_constructs.name: TestDistributionStructure
key_constructs.purpose: Asserts that every distribution has required keys, that toolset probabilities fall within (0,100], and descriptions are non-empty.
key_constructs.reasoning: Enforces data hygiene for DISTRIBUTIONS; changing the shape or value ranges will fail these assertions.

key_behaviors: asserts get_distribution returns expected structures
checks list_distributions returns a non-identical but equal mapping
validates membership via validate_distribution
ensures sampling returns at least one toolset and honors 100% probabilities

pitfalls.mistake: Treating probabilistic sampling as deterministic in tests
pitfalls.consequence: Flaky tests if assertions assume exact outcomes from random draws.
pitfalls.prevention: Tests here use repeated sampling or deterministic checks (e.g., distributions with 100%) rather than asserting exact random sets.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering probability validation to include 0 or >100 without updating tests
pitfalls.consequence: TestDistributionStructure.test_probabilities_are_valid_range will fail because it expects 0 < prob <= 100.
pitfalls.prevention: If probability semantics change, update test assertions accordingly.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or renaming distribution names referenced in tests.expected list
pitfalls.consequence: TestGetDistribution.test_all_named_distributions_exist will fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Synchronize tests when removing/renaming distributions.

key_sections: TestGetDistribution: confirms get_distribution lookup and that the expected distributions are present
TestSampleToolsetsFromDistribution: examines unknown name handling, 100% probability behavior, minimal distribution, and fallback guarantee
TestDistributionStructure: enforces distribution data hygiene
skip_unless_needed: Import block and trivial one-line assertions (quick to scan)

relevant_snippets.description: Test default distribution has 100% and asserts all toolsets are present in a sample.
Repeated sampling on the 'reasoning' distribution to exercise fallback guarantee.
Checks that every distribution has description and toolsets and that probabilities are within the allowed range.

related_files.relationship: Direct test target — validates the distribution data and sampling behavior implemented there.
related_files.reason_to_check: When changing sampling semantics or distribution data, update and re-run these tests.

related_files.relationship: Contains validate_toolset used during sampling; tests implicitly rely on its behavior (valid/invalid toolsets).
related_files.reason_to_check: If validate_toolset behavior or API changes, tests may need to be adjusted to account for different validation results.

data_constants_to_check: DISTRIBUTIONS keys and probability integer ranges

hermes_cli/auth.py.short_description: Multi-provider auth subsystem: provider registry, API-key and OAuth handling, and persisted auth state.
hermes_cli/auth.py.description: This module implements Hermes Agent's multi-provider authentication system. It centralizes provider metadata (PROVIDER_REGISTRY), facilities for resolving credentials for API-key providers and various OAuth/OIDC flows (Nous portal, OpenAI Codex, etc.), and logic to persist and load per-provider state from a user auth store (e.g., ~/.hermes/auth.json). It contains heuristics and probes for provider-specific quirks (e.g., Kimi key prefix routing, Z.AI multi-endpoint probing and caching), helpers to detect usable secrets from environment or CLI, and small OS-specific helpers (gh CLI token lookup). The module is the authoritative place for provider configuration, credential discovery priority rules, and caching of provider state used by runtime provider resolution code elsewhere (hermes_cli.runtime_provider and agent adapters).

key_constructs.name: ProviderConfig
key_constructs.purpose: Dataclass describing a known inference provider and how to authenticate against it.
key_constructs.reasoning: Providers vary by auth_type, env var names, and base URL overrides; a typed config centralizes those differences so other functions can uniformly resolve credentials and fallbacks.

key_constructs.name: PROVIDER_REGISTRY
key_constructs.purpose: Dictionary registry of ProviderConfig entries keyed by provider id.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used as the single source of truth for known/default provider metadata (ids, auth types, env var mappings). Respect when adding/resolving providers.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_kimi_base_url
key_constructs.purpose: Return correct Kimi/Moonshot base URL depending on API key prefix and explicit env override.
key_constructs.reasoning: Kimi issues different key prefixes that require different base URLs; this helper encapsulates that mapping and respects explicit environment overrides.

key_constructs.name: _gh_cli_candidates
key_constructs.purpose: Discover candidate gh CLI binary paths for token extraction.
key_constructs.reasoning: Allows token import from GitHub CLI installations, including common Homebrew locations, while avoiding unnecessary failures when gh is not installed.

key_constructs.name: _try_gh_cli_token
key_constructs.purpose: Attempt to obtain a GitHub token via `gh auth token` if the CLI is available.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to detect user credentials for providers that accept GH tokens; implements a safe short timeout and handles subprocess errors gracefully.

key_constructs.name: has_usable_secret
key_constructs.purpose: Heuristically validate whether a string looks like a usable secret rather than a placeholder.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents treating placeholders like 'changeme' or '*' as valid credentials; used for env/config secret discovery.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_api_key_provider_secret
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve an API-key provider's token from provider-specific env vars or specialized handlers (e.g., copilot).
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates priority lookup for API-key providers and special-case handling (copilot uses separate module/token validation).

key_constructs.name: ZAI_ENDPOINTS
key_constructs.purpose: List of candidate Z.AI endpoints with metadata for probing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Z.AI has multiple region/plan endpoints; probes are required to determine which endpoint a key works against.

key_constructs.name: detect_zai_endpoint
key_constructs.purpose: Probe multiple Z.AI endpoints to find one that accepts the provided API key.
key_constructs.reasoning: Network probing is necessary because different keys are scoped to different Z.AI endpoints; this function returns the first successful endpoint.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_zai_base_url
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve Z.AI base URL by honoring env overrides, cached detected endpoint (key-hash keyed), or probing candidates.
key_constructs.reasoning: Avoids repeated slow probes by caching results in provider state persisted to auth store; respects explicit GLM_BASE_URL overrides and caches per-key hash.

key_constructs.name: AuthError
key_constructs.purpose: Structured exception type carrying provider/context-specific codes and UX hints (e.g., relogin_required).
key_constructs.reasoning: Higher-level code uses this to map errors to actionable messages; handlers inspect code/relogin_required to display guidance.

key_constructs.name: format_auth_error
key_constructs.purpose: Convert AuthError (and other errors) to user-facing messages with provider-specific guidance.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes UX mapping for common auth failure modes (subscription_required, insufficient_credits, etc.).

key_constructs.name: _load_auth_store
key_constructs.purpose: Load the persisted auth.json state from HERMES_HOME (private store).
key_constructs.reasoning: Auth state persistence is cross-process and must be locked; callers rely on this to inspect cached provider state like detected Z.AI endpoints.

key_constructs.name: _load_provider_state
key_constructs.purpose: Return provider-specific state from the loaded auth store.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by helpers to read/write provider-scoped cached metadata (e.g., Z.AI detected endpoint).

key_constructs.name: _save_provider_state
key_constructs.purpose: Persist provider-specific state back into auth store and flush to disk with locking.
key_constructs.reasoning: Maintains a durable mapping (like key-hash → detected endpoint) to avoid repeated network probes.

key_constructs.name: _token_fingerprint
key_constructs.purpose: Create a short hash fingerprint of tokens for telemetry without exposing token contents.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to log or compare tokens in a non-sensitive way. Avoids leaking secrets in logs/telemetry.

key_behaviors: resolves runtime credentials for requested providers
imports or probes provider endpoints and caches results
validates and normalizes API keys and base URLs
maps low-level provider errors to actionable UX messages
persists provider state (auth.json) with file locking

pitfalls.mistake: Pre-fill provider arguments with ProviderConfig defaults at call sites
pitfalls.consequence: Environment variable overrides (e.g., HERMES_PORTAL_BASE_URL) are bypassed and never take effect
pitfalls.prevention: Pass None when CLI flags are absent so callee's priority chain (CLI > env > default) can run

pitfalls.mistake: Treat placeholder strings or short tokens as usable secrets
pitfalls.consequence: Authentication routines will attempt to use invalid credentials leading to confusing failures
pitfalls.prevention: Use has_usable_secret to gate what is considered a valid candidate and prefer credential pools or authenticated probes

pitfalls.mistake: Ignoring cached provider detection (e.g., Z.AI detected_endpoint) and re-probing every start
pitfalls.consequence: Startup becomes slow and may hit network timeouts repeatedly; redundant operations
pitfalls.prevention: Respect cached state stored in auth.json keyed by key-hash; only re-probe if no cache or key changed

pitfalls.mistake: Importing legacy/CLI tokens without checking expiry
pitfalls.consequence: Expired tokens can be reported as valid and leave users stuck; stale tokens may cause silent failures
pitfalls.prevention: Validate token expiry (e.g., JWT exp) before importing and prefer credential pool entries as authoritative

pitfalls.mistake: Logging raw tokens or returning secrets in exceptions
pitfalls.consequence: Secret leakage in logs or telemetry
pitfalls.prevention: Use _token_fingerprint for telemetry and avoid exposing raw credential bytes

key_sections: PROVIDER_REGISTRY: canonical provider metadata and env var mappings
_resolve_api_key_provider_secret: primary API-key lookup flow and special-cases (copilot)
_resolve_kimi_base_url: Kimi-specific routing logic
ZAI_ENDPOINTS / detect_zai_endpoint / _resolve_zai_base_url: multi-endpoint probing and caching
AuthError / format_auth_error: mapping provider errors to UX messages
_load_auth_store, _load_provider_state, _save_provider_state: persistent auth store interactions
skip_unless_needed: _gh_cli_candidates and _try_gh_cli_token: only needed when handling GitHub CLI token imports
low-level file locking imports (fcntl/msvcrt) unless modifying store persistence

test_functions: multiple tests in tests/test_cli_provider_resolution.py that monkeypatch resolve_nous_runtime_credentials and fetch_nous_models

relevant_snippets.description: Validates that resolve_runtime_provider uses credential pool and preserves provider/base_url behavior (exercises auth store and credential selection code paths).
Exercises Nous model flow helpers by monkeypatching resolve_nous_runtime_credentials and related auth helpers.

related_files.relationship: CLI entrypoint that uses this module to implement commands such as `hermes auth`/`hermes model` and to pre-load HERMES_HOME; auth flows here are tightly coupled to main's model flow and profile handling
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to provider defaults, env var precedence, or persistence path must be coordinated with main's profile/env loading and user-facing messages.

related_files.relationship: Higher-level orchestration module that calls into auth.* functions to obtain runtime credentials for model calls; tests in the repo exercise resolve_runtime_provider hooking into auth resolution
related_files.reason_to_check: Modifying how runtime credentials are formatted, metadata keys, or caching semantics can break runtime_provider expectations.

related_files.relationship: Provider adapter that expects base_url/api_key shapes produced by auth resolution; auth resolves anthopic-specific base_url overrides
related_files.reason_to_check: When changing anthopic-specific resolution or normalization, ensure adapter still receives expected api_mode, base_url shapes.

insights.title: Auto-detect Z.AI endpoint by probing and cache results
insights.problem: Credential pool seeder and runtime credential resolver hardcoded a single Z.AI endpoint causing keys for other Z.AI endpoints (Coding Plan / CN) to fail.
insights.root_cause: Different Z.AI API regions/paths exist and some keys only work against certain endpoints; hardcoded base URL broke those keys.
insights.solution: Added _resolve_zai_base_url that respects GLM_BASE_URL env, probes candidate endpoints via detect_zai_endpoint(), caches detected endpoint keyed by API-key hash in provider state (auth.json), and falls back to default if probes fail. Wired into credential pool seeder and resolve_api_key_provider_credentials().
insights.lesson: When providers have multiple possible endpoints, probe and cache the correct endpoint per key and persist that mapping to avoid repeating slow probes. Respect explicit environment overrides.

insights.title: Respect HERMES_PORTAL_BASE_URL env var during Nous login
insights.problem: Nous login always used production portal URL because _login_nous passed pconfig.portal_base_url as fallback forcing downstream logic to skip env var check.
insights.root_cause: Code passed production default when CLI flag not provided, preempting env var fallback chain.
insights.solution: Pass None when CLI arg is not provided so downstream function checks env vars (HERMES_PORTAL_BASE_URL, NOUS_PORTAL_BASE_URL) before falling back to DEFAULT_NOUS_PORTAL_URL. Also applied to inference_base_url.
insights.lesson_learned: When wiring configuration fallbacks, prefer leaving None to allow downstream logic to check env vars and defaults in a single place.

insights.title: Import codex CLI tokens only if not expired; check pool first for Codex status
insights.problem: Importing expired tokens from ~/.codex/auth.json could leave users stuck; credential pool tokens weren't considered in get_codex_auth_status leading to stale token usage.
insights.root_cause: _import_codex_cli_tokens didn't validate token expiry. get_codex_auth_status only used legacy provider state and ignored tokens present in credential pool.
insights.solution: Reject expired tokens in _import_codex_cli_tokens by checking JWT expiry. Update get_codex_auth_status to prefer credential pool entries (load_pool('openai-codex')) and fallback to legacy state. Ensure _login_openai_codex verifies token freshness before reporting success.
insights.lesson_learned: When importing credentials from external files or legacy state, always validate expiry and prefer the authoritative credential pool; do not report success on expired tokens.

insights.title: Replace 'hermes login' references with 'hermes auth'
insights.problem: User messages and documentation referenced deprecated 'hermes login' command.
insights.root_cause: Command rename/deprecation left stale references.
insights.solution: Replace user-facing strings and docs to recommend 'hermes auth' and adapt related flows.
insights.lesson_learned: Keep CLI messages and docs aligned with current commands; centralize messages where possible.

insights.title: Actionable error when Codex refresh token reused
insights.problem: When a Codex refresh token had been consumed by another client, Hermes raised a generic 401/AuthError with no recovery instructions.
insights.root_cause: OAuth error code refresh_token_reused was not recognized and mapped to a helpful message.
insights.solution: Detect code == 'refresh_token_reused' and raise AuthError with explicit recovery steps instructing user to run codex then hermes login --provider openai-codex.
insights.lesson_learned: Map specific provider error codes to actionable, user-facing guidance rather than generic errors to improve recoverability.

insights.title: Add Gemini provider to PROVIDER_REGISTRY
insights.problem: There was no first-class Gemini provider support (Google AI Studio).
insights.root_cause: New provider integration requires provider config and env var mapping.
insights.solution: Add PROVIDER_REGISTRY entry for 'gemini' with inference_base_url, api_key env vars (GOOGLE_API_KEY, GEMINI_API_KEY), base url env var GEMINI_BASE_URL, and alias mappings for google→gemini in resolve_provider.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding a provider ensure auth config, env var aliases, and resolve logic are in sync; provide base_url override support for custom endpoints.

insights.title: Nous OAuth login: surface subscription-required errors
insights.problem: When refresh failed with subscription_required, users weren't guided to subscribe.
insights.root_cause: refresh_nous_oauth_from_state exceptions were not specially handled.
insights.solution: Catch AuthError with code 'subscription_required' and print user-friendly subscribe URL and exit.
insights.lesson_learned: Surface provider-specific auth errors as actionable messages instead of generic crashes; include direct URLs when possible.

insights.title: Provide config diagnostics when provider resolution fails
insights.problem: resolve_provider raised 'Unknown provider' with no hint when misconfigured YAML caused provider entries to be invisible.
insights.root_cause: Malformed config structure (e.g., custom_providers as dict) led to provider lookup failures and poor error messaging.
insights.solution: Added _get_config_hint_for_unknown_provider() calling validate_config_structure() to produce helpful diagnostics appended to Unknown provider errors; updated user-facing error text to recommend 'hermes doctor'.
insights.lesson_learned: Augment low-level errors with higher-level diagnostics when config problems are a common root cause; guide users to a 'doctor' tool for remediation.

insights.title: Allow env var overrides for Nous portal/inference URLs
insights.problem: Env var overrides for portal/inference URLs couldn't take effect because call site pre-filled parameters with pconfig defaults.
insights.root_cause: Call site passed getattr(args,..) or pconfig.*; pconfig defaults were truthy, masking environment variables inside lower-level function.
insights.solution: Pass None from call site when CLI flags are not present so the callee's priority chain (CLI flag > env var > pconfig default) works correctly.
insights.lesson_learned: Respect the called function's internal priority chain: pass through None to allow environment-driven overrides; avoid pre-resolving defaults at call sites.

insights.title: Show cache pricing if supported when selecting models
insights.problem: Users couldn't see cache read pricing in model selection UI even when provider exposes it.
insights.root_cause: Pricing handling initially only included prompt/completion pricing, not cache pricing.
insights.solution: Updated model selection UI to include cache pricing column when present (interacts with hermes_cli.models pricing functions).
insights.lesson_learned: When adding pricing UI, propagate changes across UI and provider helpers; ensure non-blocking fetch and graceful fallback on failure.

insights.title: Register Hugging Face provider in provider registry
insights.problem: HF provider was not recognized in provider registry and alias resolution.
insights.root_cause: Previously no PROVIDER_REGISTRY entry for huggingface.
insights.solution: Add ProviderConfig for 'huggingface' and alias mappings ('hf', 'hugging-face', etc.).
insights.lesson_learned: When adding a provider, update both registry and alias resolution to support SHORTHAND values and environment detection.

tests/test_cli_provider_resolution.py.short_description: Unit tests for CLI provider resolution, model flow, and runtime credential interaction.
tests/test_cli_provider_resolution.py.description: This test module exercises Hermes CLI behavior around provider/runtime resolution, model selection flows (notably Nous and Codex paths), and interactions with runtime_provider and auth helpers. It isolates the CLI import (cli.HermesCLI) to avoid cross-test leakage by saving/restoring certain modules and installing stubs for interactive libraries (prompt_toolkit). The tests heavily patch hermes_cli.runtime_provider and hermes_cli.auth functions to simulate network, token, and config states so they can validate HermesCLI's decision logic without performing real network auth or file operations. Many tests validate that credentials are lazily resolved, that runtime resolution failures are retried and not sticky, and that provider-specific model normalization rules are respected.

key_constructs.name: _reset_modules
key_constructs.purpose: Helper to remove modules with given prefixes from sys.modules to allow clean re-import of CLI modules
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures test isolation across xdist workers and prevents stale mocks from leaking between tests.

key_constructs.name: _restore_cli_and_tool_modules
key_constructs.purpose: pytest fixture that saves and restores modules under ('tools','cli','run_agent') around each test
key_constructs.reasoning: Automates module state snapshot/restore to avoid cross-test contamination; declared autouse so it runs for every test in the file.

key_constructs.name: _install_prompt_toolkit_stubs
key_constructs.purpose: Install minimal prompt_toolkit module stubs into sys.modules for tests to avoid pulling the real dependency
key_constructs.reasoning: CLI imports prompt_toolkit; tests run in environments where the real dependency may not be installed. Stubs let CLI construct without UI side-effects.

key_constructs.name: _import_cli
key_constructs.purpose: Import the top-level CLI module fresh after clearing certain modules and ensuring prompt_toolkit is available
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides fresh import state for each test so configuration constants and CLI initialization patterns are deterministic.

key_constructs.name: test_hermes_cli_init_does_not_eagerly_resolve_runtime_provider
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies HermesCLI.__init__ does not call runtime provider resolution during construction
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures lazy credential resolution to avoid unnecessary network/auth work on CLI start.

key_constructs.name: test_runtime_resolution_failure_is_not_sticky
key_constructs.purpose: Ensures a transient runtime resolution failure doesn't permanently block later successful resolutions
key_constructs.reasoning: Validates retry behavior and that subsequent attempts re-invoke resolve_runtime_provider.

key_behaviors: verifies lazy resolution of runtime credentials
ensures provider/model normalization logic preserves explicit user choices
tests Nous model flow messaging and managed-tool defaults
validates credential fallback behavior (credential pool vs auth store)

pitfalls.mistake: Relying on global module state between tests
pitfalls.consequence: Mocks and patches leak causing nondeterministic failures
pitfalls.prevention: Use the provided _restore_cli_and_tool_modules fixture and _import_cli to re-import CLI cleanly

pitfalls.mistake: Not installing prompt_toolkit stubs when the real library is missing
pitfalls.consequence: Import-time ModuleNotFoundError in tests
pitfalls.prevention: Call _install_prompt_toolkit_stubs through _import_cli which ensures stubs exist if prompt_toolkit is absent

key_sections: _restore_cli_and_tool_modules fixture: understand module snapshot/restore semantics
prompt_toolkit stubs: used to avoid UI dependencies in tests
early tests: lazy resolution and retry semantics (lines ~40-120)
skip_unless_needed: larger mocked scenarios that exercise model selection UI

relevant_snippets.description: Module isolation logic and fixtures that are critical for test reliability.
Tests validating lazy runtime resolution, retry semantics, and rebuild-on-routing-change behavior.

related_files.relationship: Contains HermesCLI implementation and model flow helpers that the tests import and exercise indirectly
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to HermesCLI signature, initialization behavior, or attribute names will break these tests

related_files.relationship: Monkepatched heavily by tests to simulate provider resolution outcomes; runtime_provider API must remain stable for tests to function
related_files.reason_to_check: If resolve_runtime_provider or its return shape changes, tests must be updated accordingly

related_files.relationship: Test monkeypatches several auth.* functions (resolve_nous_runtime_credentials, fetch_nous_models) to simulate provider flows; auth module provides behaviors verified by these tests
related_files.reason_to_check: If auth helpers change names, semantics or return shapes, tests must adjust their monkeypatches

data_constants_to_check: ENV vars used in tests: HERMES_ENABLE_NOUS_MANAGED_TOOLS, LLM_MODEL, OPENAI_MODEL, HERMES_INFERENCE_PROVIDER

acp_adapter/server.py.short_description: ACP protocol Agent implementation that exposes Hermes Agent over the Agent Client Protocol
acp_adapter/server.py.description:
