This module implements an acp.Agent wrapper around the Hermes AI Agent so the agent can be driven remotely via the Agent Client Protocol (ACP). It translates ACP concepts (sessions, content blocks, MCP server descriptors, permission requests, available commands, and streaming updates) into calls against an in-process Hermes agent and conversely translates agent events back into ACP session updates. The class exists so editors/IDEs/other clients that implement ACP can drive Hermes without using the CLI or gateway platform adapters.

Key responsibilities: mapping ACP lifecycle methods (initialize, authenticate, new_session, load_session, resume_session, prompt, cancel, fork_session, list_sessions) to the SessionManager and the in-process agent; extracting text from ACP content blocks; registering ACP-provided MCP servers into Hermes tools; advertising supported slash commands and updating the client; running the synchronous agent.run_conversation in a threadpool to avoid blocking the async ACP server. The implementation takes care to safely import optional tooling (model_tools, tools.mcp_tool) and degrades with warnings if optional integration fails, so the server remains available even if tool registration fails.

How it fits into the system: this file is the bridge between the external ACP clients and the Hermes core. It depends on acp (third-party agent-client-protocol package), acp_adapter.session.SessionManager for session state, and acp_adapter.events/permissions to create callbacks that stream agent events back to the ACP client. It also integrates with the tools system by registering ACP MCP servers and refreshing the agent's tool surface. The module is sensitive to runtime environment (detection of a configured model provider for authentication) and uses a threadpool executor to run blocking agent code concurrently with the async ACP server loop.

key_constructs.name: _executor
key_constructs.purpose: ThreadPoolExecutor used to run synchronous agent execution in parallel without blocking the async loop
key_constructs.reasoning: Agent.run_conversation is synchronous and potentially long-running; using this executor prevents blocking asyncio. Edits that change concurrency or executor behavior must preserve non-blocking behavior and thread-safety.

key_constructs.name: HERMES_VERSION
key_constructs.purpose: Holds the Hermes CLI package version if importable, otherwise a default string
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to advertise agent_info in InitializeResponse. Keep this import pattern (try/except) because hermes_cli may not be available in all environments.

key_constructs.name: _extract_text
key_constructs.purpose: Extracts plain text from a list of ACP content blocks, joining text parts with newlines
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to convert ACP prompt blocks to a single user text string for the agent. Changes must preserve support for TextContentBlock and safe handling of other block types with 'text' attributes.

key_constructs.name: HermesACPAgent
key_constructs.purpose: Core ACP Agent implementation that maps ACP session and prompt interactions to Hermes agent behavior
key_constructs.reasoning: All ACP lifecycle hooks are implemented as methods on this class. Any change that alters signatures, return types, or the use of session_manager must respect acp.Agent expectations and the SessionManager API.

key_constructs.name: on_connect
key_constructs.purpose: Store the acp.Client connection for later session update calls and log connection
key_constructs.reasoning: The stored connection is used throughout to stream updates back to the client; ensure that connection is set and cleared appropriately to avoid Nones.

key_constructs.name: _register_session_mcp_servers
key_constructs.purpose: Register ACP-provided MCP servers (stdio/http/sse) into Hermes via tools.mcp_tool and refresh available tool definitions
key_constructs.reasoning: This function imports and calls external modules (tools.mcp_tool, model_tools). It must run in an executor context or be safe to call from asyncio (it uses asyncio.to_thread). It also updates state.agent.tools and valid_tool_names and calls an invalidate callback if available.

key_constructs.name: initialize
key_constructs.purpose: ACP initialize handler: advertises agent capabilities, supported protocol version, and optional auth methods detected from runtime provider configuration
key_constructs.reasoning: This shapes the initial capabilities presented to ACP clients. It relies on detect_provider() to supply auth options when available. Keep response shapes compatible with acp.schema.InitializeResponse.

key_constructs.name: authenticate
key_constructs.purpose: ACP authenticate handler that returns success only if a provider is configured
key_constructs.reasoning: It uses has_provider() to decide whether to accept authentication; changing this logic may alter which clients can authenticate.

key_constructs.name: new_session
key_constructs.purpose: Create a new Session via SessionManager, optionally register MCP servers, advertise commands, and return the new session id
key_constructs.reasoning: Session creation flows into many subsequent operations; ensure session_manager.create_session semantics are preserved.

key_constructs.name: load_session
key_constructs.purpose: Load an existing session and update its cwd; registers MCP servers and advertises commands
key_constructs.reasoning: If the session is missing, it logs and returns None — callers expect None to indicate failure to load.

key_constructs.name: resume_session
key_constructs.purpose: Resume a session, creating a new one if the requested id is not found; registers MCP servers and advertises commands
key_constructs.reasoning: This method differs from load_session by creating a new session as fallback; edits must keep this fallback behavior intact unless intentionally changing semantics.

key_constructs.name: cancel
key_constructs.purpose: Signal cancellation for a session: set its cancel_event and attempt to call agent.interrupt if available
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to abort running runs; must preserve the setting of cancel_event and safe calling of interrupt to avoid lost cancellation or exceptions.

key_constructs.name: fork_session
key_constructs.purpose: Create a forked copy of an existing session (via SessionManager) and register MCP servers for the fork
key_constructs.reasoning: It notifies clients by advertising commands for the new forked session; changes may impact session lineage behavior.

key_constructs.name: list_sessions
key_constructs.purpose: Return a list of stored sessions as ACP SessionInfo objects
key_constructs.reasoning: Relies on SessionManager.list_sessions; the structure of returned session dicts (session_id, cwd) must remain compatible.

key_constructs.name: prompt
key_constructs.purpose: Primary prompt entry: convert ACP prompt to text, handle slash commands locally, prepare callbacks, run the synchronous Hermes agent in a thread, persist history, and stream final response and usage
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the most complex and critical method: it sets up callbacks (tool progress, thinking, step, message, approval), modifies a terminal_tool approval callback, runs agent.run_conversation inside the executor, handles exceptions, persists session history, and returns PromptResponse including usage. Edits must be careful with concurrency, callback lifetimes, and restoring previous approval callbacks.

key_constructs.name: _available_commands
key_constructs.purpose: Build a list of AvailableCommand objects from the module's _ADVERTISED_COMMANDS list
key_constructs.reasoning: This is used when advertising slash commands to clients; maintain mapping between spec entries and ACP objects (UnstructuredCommandInput when input_hint is present).

key_constructs.name: _send_available_commands_update
key_constructs.purpose: Send AvailableCommandsUpdate to the connected ACP client for a session
key_constructs.reasoning: It uses the stored _conn to session_update; network errors are logged and suppressed. Ensure connection may be None and is checked.

key_behaviors: creates sessions via SessionManager when requested
loads and resumes sessions, updating working directory
runs Hermes agent on user prompts and streams back events
registers ACP MCP servers into Hermes tools
advertises supported slash commands to ACP clients
supports cancellation of long-running agent runs

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying agent.run_conversation to run in the event loop or removing the executor
pitfalls.consequence: Blocking the asyncio loop, causing the ACP server to stop responding to other clients or heartbeats
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the call inside loop.run_in_executor or otherwise ensure it runs off the main event loop

pitfalls.mistake: Changing how the approval callback is set on tools.terminal_tool without restoring the previous callback
pitfalls.consequence: Global state leak: later prompts may use the wrong approval callback, causing permission flows to behave incorrectly across sessions
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve previous_approval_cb and restore it in all control paths (including exceptions/finally) as current code does

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming self._conn is always present and not checking for None before sending updates
pitfalls.consequence: AttributeError or failed updates when no client is connected
pitfalls.prevention: Always check self._conn before calling session_update (the code already does this at several points)

pitfalls.mistake: Making heavy imports (model_tools, tools.mcp_tool) synchronous on the event loop without using asyncio.to_thread
pitfalls.consequence: Potential event loop blocking during tool registration
pitfalls.prevention: Continue to perform heavy/IO-bound imports/calls inside threads (asyncio.to_thread) or ensure they are fast

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the shape of InitializeResponse, PromptResponse or other acp.schema types
pitfalls.consequence: ACP clients may reject the handshake or fail to parse responses
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the expected schema fields and types when constructing these response objects

key_sections: prompt: core prompt flow, callback setup, executor usage, history persistence
_register_session_mcp_servers: MCP server registration and tool refresh logic
initialize/authenticate: how agent capabilities and auth methods are exposed to clients
_available_commands/_send_available_commands_update: command advertising logic
skip_unless_needed: module-level constants and SLASH command lists
logging statements and basic passthrough session handlers (list_sessions, fork_session) if only interested in prompt execution

related_files.relationship: SessionManager implementation used to create/load/fork/save session state referenced by the ACP agent
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to session lifecycle here must be coordinated with SessionManager semantics (session ids, history persistence, cancel_event)

related_files.relationship: Factory functions for callbacks (make_message_cb, make_step_cb, etc.) used to stream agent events back to ACP clients
related_files.reason_to_check: Modifications to event payloads or callback expectations require updating how prompt constructs and uses these callbacks

related_files.relationship: detect_provider and has_provider functions are used to decide auth options and whether to accept authentication
related_files.reason_to_check: Auth behavior changes affect initialize() and authenticate() responses advertised to ACP clients

related_files.relationship: register_mcp_servers is called to configure MCP servers described by ACP and integrate them into Hermes tooling
related_files.reason_to_check: If MCP server registration behavior or config schema changes, this module must be coordinated, since _register_session_mcp_servers constructs config_map expected by register_mcp_servers

related_files.relationship: get_tool_definitions is used to refresh the agent's tool surface after MCP registration
related_files.reason_to_check: Tool definitions and enabled/disabled toolset handling affect the agent.tools assignments and the set of valid_tool_names

tests_to_run: python -m pytest -q (run unit tests; CI ignores integration/e2e by default)
If modifying ACP behavior, run integration/e2e tests if available (pytest tests/e2e/ -v)
data_constants_to_check: acp.PROTOCOL_VERSION (ensure InitializeResponse.protocol_version aligns with supported protocol)

hermes_cli/doctor.py.short_description: CLI diagnostic command that inspects Hermes installation, config, and runtime health
hermes_cli/doctor.py.description:
This module implements the `hermes doctor` command used by the CLI to diagnose installation, configuration, and runtime issues. It collects environment information (Python version, virtualenv), presence of required and optional packages, configuration files and structure, authentication status for external providers, directory layout and important files under HERMES_HOME, SQLite state store health (including WAL checkpointing), external tools (git, codex CLI), and platform-specific checks such as systemd user linger for the gateway service. The command prints a colored, user-facing report using helper printing functions and can optionally attempt to auto-fix some issues when run with a fix flag.

Why it exists: doctor centralizes sanity checks and common remediation steps so users can quickly troubleshoot setup problems. It also ties into the broader hermes_cli.config validation and migration logic and gives visibility into provider credentials (via environment and auth helpers). The command intentionally mirrors the runtime context of the interactive CLI by setting HERMES_INTERACTIVE and by reading both per-user and project .env files.

How it fits: It is a user-facing CLI helper that depends on several internal modules (hermes_cli.config, hermes_cli.gateway, hermes_cli.auth, hermes_cli.colors) and optional plugin configuration (plugins.memory.honcho). The command both reads state (files, DB, env) and, when requested, performs deterministic mutating operations (create missing directories, create files from example, migrate config keys, perform sqlite wal checkpoint), so edits to this file must respect what other parts of the system expect to be present under HERMES_HOME and the shape of config.yaml.

Non-obvious behavior / historical context: The module loads dotenv files at import-time (user and project .env) and falls back to latin-1 on UnicodeDecodeError. Commit history shows a past bug fix where doctor printed HonchoClientConfig attributes using an old attribute name (memory_mode -> recall_mode), and a later feature added config structure diagnostics. This indicates the doctor code must remain aligned with the shapes of config objects and validators in other modules.

key_constructs.name: PROJECT_ROOT
key_constructs.purpose: Project root path determined by hermes_cli.config.get_project_root
key_constructs.reasoning: Used as a fallback location for configuration (.env, cli-config.yaml etc.); editors must preserve how this is computed because doctor uses it as a fallback and to locate example config files.

key_constructs.name: HERMES_HOME
key_constructs.purpose: User data home path determined by hermes_cli.config.get_hermes_home
key_constructs.reasoning: Primary authoritative path for agent data (memories, sessions, config); changes to how HERMES_HOME is used will impact many checks and any auto-fixes that create files/directories here.

key_constructs.name: _DHH
key_constructs.purpose: User-facing display path produced by hermes_constants.display_hermes_home()
key_constructs.reasoning: Used in output messages to present a friendly path (may include profile paths), so output strings rely on this formatting.

key_constructs.name: _PROVIDER_ENV_HINTS
key_constructs.purpose: Tuple of environment variable names used to detect configured model/auth providers
key_constructs.reasoning: Doctor scans env file content for any of these keys to decide if provider credentials or endpoints are present; changing this list affects provider detection behavior.

key_constructs.name: _has_provider_env_config
key_constructs.purpose: Return True when .env content contains provider auth/base URL keys
key_constructs.reasoning: A small helper used when inspecting the .env file to produce user guidance; it is intentionally simple (string containment) so edits should preserve that behavior unless provider hint semantics change.

key_constructs.name: _honcho_is_configured_for_doctor
key_constructs.purpose: Detect whether Honcho client config is present/enabled so doctor can surface honcho tool availability
key_constructs.reasoning: This function imports a plugin module at runtime and tolerates exceptions; it is purposely defensive because the Honcho plugin may not be installed. Maintainers must preserve the try/except behavior and the exact boolean semantics used by other doctor logic.

key_constructs.name: _apply_doctor_tool_availability_overrides
key_constructs.purpose: When Honcho is configured, mark the 'honcho' tool as available in the doctor's tool availability report
key_constructs.reasoning: This function mutates provided availability lists and is used to harmonize the CLI tool availability check with plugin configuration. It must keep the contract of returning (available, unavailable) with unchanged semantics for other tools.

key_constructs.name: check_ok
key_constructs.purpose: Print a success line with green tick and optional detail
key_constructs.reasoning: Used throughout for consistent, colorized output. Other helper prints (check_warn/check_fail/check_info) provide matched UX; changing these will alter CLI output consistency and tests that rely on messages.

key_constructs.name: _check_gateway_service_linger
key_constructs.purpose: Linux-specific check that warns when systemd linger is disabled and offers remediation
key_constructs.reasoning: Imports hermes_cli.gateway helpers and returns early on import errors; it queries systemd unit path and linger status and appends to issues list when relevant. Because it touches OS-specific behavior (loginctl enable-linger), edits must be careful about platform detection and non-fatal import errors.

key_constructs.name: run_doctor
key_constructs.purpose: Main entry point that runs the diagnostic checks and optionally auto-fixes issues when args.fix is set
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the primary function invoked by the CLI. It orchestrates checks across environment, packages, config files, auth providers, directory layout, DB health, external tools, and more. It performs mutations when should_fix is True (file/dir creation, config migration, WAL checkpoint), so changes have side effects and must preserve the semantics and safety precautions (e.g., non-interactive auto-fix behavior controlled by args).

key_behaviors: prints a multi-section diagnostic report about environment and configuration
reads user and project .env files and reports presence of provider keys
validates and can migrate config.yaml and stale root keys
inspects directory structure under HERMES_HOME and creates missing directories/files when --fix is supplied
examines SQLite state.db and performs WAL checkpoint when --fix is supplied

pitfalls.mistake: Moving dotenv loading out of module import without preserving fallback encoding behavior
pitfalls.consequence: Some setups with non-UTF-8 .env files will raise UnicodeDecodeError or mis-detect provider envs; tests or CLI runs may behave differently
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the current load_dotenv sequence with the latin-1 fallback or ensure equivalent handling if changed

pitfalls.mistake: Converting lazy imports (plugins.memory.honcho or hermes_cli.gateway) into unconditional top-level imports
pitfalls.consequence: Environments lacking optional plugins will raise import errors at module import-time; doctor should remain tolerant of optional dependencies
pitfalls.prevention: Retain try/except around optional imports and keep the defensive behavior used in this file

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the file/directory creation logic to write different paths or change when auto-fix runs
pitfalls.consequence: Auto-fix may create files in unexpected locations or fail silently; existing expectations about created artifacts (config.yaml in HERMES_HOME, SOUL.md template format) will be broken
pitfalls.prevention: Respect HERMES_HOME usage and only perform mutations when should_fix is True; maintain the same file encodings and default templates

pitfalls.mistake: Changing printed messages or color output tokens without coordinating other CLI output consumers or tests
pitfalls.consequence: Automated scripts, tests, or documentation that parse doctor output could break
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve existing check_* formatting or adjust consumers/tests accordingly

key_sections: Top-level dotenv loading: determines how env vars are loaded (affects provider detection)
run_doctor: main orchestration, sets HERMES_INTERACTIVE and loops through checks
Checks for config.yaml: migration & stale root-level key rewriter (non-obvious because it mutates config)
_check_gateway_service_linger: platform-specific systemd handling
skip_unless_needed: Helper printing functions (check_ok/check_warn/check_fail/check_info) — simple formatting
_apply_doctor_tool_availability_overrides — only relevant if changing tool availability logic

related_files.relationship: Doctor reads get_project_root/get_hermes_home/get_env_path and invokes config validators/migrations
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to config loading, format, migration versioning, or validate_config_structure API will affect doctor behavior and output

related_files.relationship: Doctor calls gateway helpers for systemd linger detection and unit path resolution
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to systemd unit naming, linger detection, or platform detection semantics will require updating the doctor's corresponding checks

related_files.relationship: Doctor imports HonchoClientConfig to decide honcho availability and display its status
related_files.reason_to_check: Renames of config attributes (e.g., memory_mode -> recall_mode) or API changes in HonchoClientConfig require doctor updates to avoid misleading output or import errors

related_files.relationship: Doctor uses Colors and color() for terminal output formatting
related_files.reason_to_check: If color codes/formatting functions change, doctor's printed output may need adjustment

related_files.relationship: Doctor uses display_hermes_home() and OPENROUTER_MODELS_URL constants
related_files.reason_to_check: Altered constants or display formatting will change user-facing messages

tests_to_run: Run any CLI-level tests that cover hermes_cli (if present)

insights.title: Use recall_mode instead of memory_mode for HonchoClientConfig display
insights.problem: Doctor printed Honcho client mode using outdated attribute memory_mode which no longer exists.
insights.root_cause: Attribute rename in HonchoClientConfig (memory_mode -> recall_mode) wasn't reflected in doctor code.
insights.solution: Replace memory_mode with recall_mode when formatting check message.
insights.lesson_learned: Keep diagnostic tools updated when underlying configs/attributes are renamed to avoid confusing output.

insights.title: Doctor now reports Config Structure issues
insights.problem: hermes doctor previously lacked structured diagnostics for malformed config.yaml.
insights.root_cause: No centralized config structure validator existed.
insights.solution: Doctor runs validate_config_structure() and prints Config Structure section with severity and hints.
insights.lesson_learned: Integrate config validation into diagnostic commands to centralize and standardize remediation steps.

tools/cronjob_tools.py.short_description: Unified cron job management tool with prompt-scanning and API boundary validation
tools/cronjob_tools.py.description:
This module exposes a single, compressed tool interface for managing scheduled cron jobs in Hermes Agent. It centralizes create/list/update/pause/resume/remove/trigger operations behind a JSON-based tool function (cronjob) intended for use by the agent (model-invoked tools) and provides small compatibility wrappers for legacy Python callers. The file intentionally performs safety checks at the API boundary: scanning cron prompts for critical prompt-injection/exfiltration patterns, validating script paths to remain within HERMES_HOME/scripts/, and pinning model provider choices where appropriate.

The module is designed to be called both programmatically (compatibility wrappers schedule_cronjob/list_cronjobs/remove_cronjob) and by the agent-tool invocation path (the JSON schema CRONJOB_SCHEMA describes the tool surface presented to models). It delegates actual job store and scheduling operations to an external cron.jobs module (imported at the top). Several helper functions encapsulate normalization and validation logic so that cron job records are stored in a stable, predictable shape and so cron-run sessions remain secure.

Key design decisions visible in the code: narrow, critical-only prompt scanning to avoid over-blocking (list of high-severity regexes); enforcing relative script paths that resolve under HERMES_HOME/scripts to eliminate arbitrary script execution; and best-effort pinning of the provider when a model name is supplied without a provider to avoid jobs drifting when user changes default model later. The module also exposes CRONJOB_SCHEMA (JSON schema) to describe the tool for model use and a small environment-based origin extraction function to record the session source that created the job.

key_constructs.name: _scan_cron_prompt
key_constructs.purpose: Check a prompt string for invisible/unicode characters and a curated set of high-severity threat regexes; returns an error string if blocked, else empty string.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the primary security gate preventing prompt-injection/exfiltration payloads in scheduled prompts. Any edits must preserve the exact behavioral contract (returning a non-empty string to block).

key_constructs.name: _validate_cron_script_path
key_constructs.purpose: Validate script path strings (API boundary) to ensure they are relative and resolve inside HERMES_HOME/scripts; returns an error string or None.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents absolute paths, ~ expansion, and directory traversal. It creates the scripts directory if missing and resolves containment; changes affect which scripts can be referenced by jobs and security posture.

key_constructs.name: _canonical_skills
key_constructs.purpose: Normalize skill parameters supplied as skill or skills into a deduplicated List[str] preserving order.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by create/update flows to canonicalize various inputs; expected to filter out empty strings and preserve first element for 'skill' field.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_model_override
key_constructs.purpose: Normalize a model override dict into a (provider, model) tuple, pinning provider to current configured provider when only model name is provided.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures created jobs are stable: if only model name supplied, it attempts to pin the current default provider from hermes_cli.config.load_config() so later default changes don't alter job routing. This is best-effort and swallows exceptions.

key_constructs.name: _format_job
key_constructs.purpose: Format internal job dict into a compact external representation used by list and responses.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the shape returned to callers and tool responses (preview, repeat display, state fields). Changes here alter the external API shape of job objects returned to callers and tools.

key_constructs.name: cronjob
key_constructs.purpose: Primary entry point: unified cron job management tool that accepts action and parameters, delegates to cron.jobs functions and returns JSON strings.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function is the core of the module: it validates inputs, applies security checks, calls cron.jobs API (create_job, list_jobs, get_job, update_job, pause_job, resume_job, trigger_job, remove_job), and returns JSON encoded responses. Any consumer (agent tools, compatibility wrappers) depends on its exact JSON output format and error messaging.

key_constructs.name: CRONJOB_SCHEMA
key_constructs.purpose: JSON-schema description of the cronjob tool surface for model/tool integration.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to expose the tool signature to model tooling; changes affect how the model is allowed to call the tool and what parameters are considered valid/required.

key_constructs.name: schedule_cronjob
key_constructs.purpose: Compatibility wrapper for creating a cron job by delegating to cronjob(action='create').
key_constructs.reasoning: Maintains backwards-compatible Python-callable API used by legacy code/tests; wrappers like this appear to be relied upon externally.

key_constructs.name: list_cronjobs
key_constructs.purpose: Compatibility wrapper for listing cron jobs by delegating to cronjob(action='list').
key_constructs.reasoning: Same compatibility role as schedule_cronjob.

key_constructs.name: remove_cronjob
key_constructs.purpose: Compatibility wrapper for removing jobs by delegating to cronjob(action='remove').
key_constructs.reasoning: Legacy caller compatibility surface.

key_constructs.name: check_cronjob_requirements
key_constructs.purpose: Check environment flags to determine whether cronjob tools can be used in the current process (interactive/gateway/exec modes).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to gate exposure of the cron tool to certain runtime contexts; depends on several HERMES_* environment variables.

key_behaviors: creates cron jobs from prompts or skills
lists existing cron jobs with compact formatting
updates job metadata and scheduling
pauses, resumes, removes, and triggers jobs
scans prompts and validates script paths for security

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or weakening _scan_cron_prompt checks or changing return type semantics
pitfalls.consequence: Cron prompts could allow prompt-injection or exfiltration payloads to run autonomously; tooling that expects non-empty string to indicate block will behave incorrectly.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the function's contract: return '' when ok, non-empty error string when blocked; retain the curated regexes and invisible-char checks.

pitfalls.mistake: Allowing absolute or ~ paths in script field or changing containment logic
pitfalls.consequence: Jobs could reference arbitrary filesystem locations, enabling remote code execution or access to secrets when cron-run executes scripts.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the relative-only policy and the containment check that resolves paths against get_hermes_home()/scripts. Do not remove the resolve() + relative_to() containment validation.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing the sys.path hack or changing how cron.jobs is imported without ensuring import path availability
pitfalls.consequence: At runtime cron.jobs functions (create_job, list_jobs, etc.) may not be importable in certain contexts leading to ImportError; tests or runtime that expect this module to be usable will fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing import approach or ensure alternate runtime import paths are compatible across environments that load this tool module.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing JSON output shapes produced by cronjob() (keys/names) without updating callers or tool schema
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks downstream consumers (agent tool invocations, UI, compatibility wrappers) that parse fields like job_id, job, schedule, repeat or message.
pitfalls.prevention: Any change to response shapes must be coordinated with the agent tool usage and schema (CRONJOB_SCHEMA) and tests verifying exact output.

key_sections: _scan_cron_prompt: security scanning of prompt input (critical to keep intact)
_validate_cron_script_path: enforces scripts directory containment and rejects absolute paths
_canonical_skills: inputs normalization for skills/skill fields
cronjob: main dispatch and JSON response formatting; delegates to cron.jobs functions
skip_unless_needed: CRONJOB_SCHEMA constant block (read when changing API/tool schema)
compatibility wrappers (schedule_cronjob, list_cronjobs, remove_cronjob) unless altering public API

related_files.relationship: Delegated scheduler implementation: create_job, get_job, list_jobs, update_job, pause_job, resume_job, trigger_job, remove_job, parse_schedule are called from this module.
related_files.reason_to_check: If job storage shape or function signatures change in cron/jobs.py (e.g., job dict keys, schedule parsing return), this file's callers and response formatting must be updated.

related_files.relationship: Configuration load used by _resolve_model_override to read current default model/provider to pin provider when only model name supplied.
related_files.reason_to_check: If the model config layout changes or load_config() raises different errors, _resolve_model_override behavior may change.

related_files.relationship: Provides get_hermes_home() used to locate ~/.hermes/scripts/ for _validate_cron_script_path.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to HERMES_HOME semantics (path type, returned object type) affect script resolution and containment logic.

data_constants_to_check: environment variables: HERMES_HOME (via hermes_constants.get_hermes_home()), HERMES_INTERACTIVE, HERMES_GATEWAY_SESSION, HERMES_EXEC_ASK, HERMES_SESSION_* env vars

gateway/session.py.short_description: Session management: session keys, session context prompt generation, PII redaction, and session metadata model.
gateway/session.py.description:
This module centralizes gateway session logic used by the messaging gateway and other agent frontend code. It provides deterministic session key construction (build_session_key) from an incoming SessionSource, models for session metadata (SessionEntry) and runtime context (SessionContext / SessionSource), and utilities for building the dynamic system prompt section injected into model requests (build_session_context_prompt). The module also contains PII redaction helpers and hashing utilities used to safely present identifiers to LLMs when required. The file is the single source of truth for how sessions are identified, how prompt context is composed and redacted, and which platforms require raw IDs (Discord) vs. can use hashed IDs (WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram).

The design separates data models (dataclasses SessionSource, SessionContext, SessionEntry) from operations on them (key building, prompt construction, hashing). SessionSource captures where a message came from (platform, chat, user, thread, optional chat topic) and supplies helper behavior (to_dict/from_dict and description). SessionContext aggregates source plus connected platforms and home channels; it is what build_session_context_prompt consumes to build an LLM-facing system prompt fragment describing the environment. The prompt generator deliberately avoids pinning a single user identity for multi-user threads (to avoid prompt cache busting) and selectively redacts PII only on platforms enumerated in _PII_SAFE_PLATFORMS.

This module also interoperates with persistent session storage (SessionStore) and with an optional SQLite-backed migration layer (hermes_state.SessionDB) — tests exercise and document expectations about the transcript read/merge behavior between JSONL transcripts and SQLite. The build_session_key function is considered a compatibility/semantic boundary: changes to its format or logic will affect persisted sessions, prompt caching, and routing. The commit history indicates an explicit UX-driven change to default thread semantics (shared thread sessions by default) which affects build_session_key and prompt building and is covered by tests in tests/gateway/test_session.py.

key_constructs.name: _now
key_constructs.purpose: Return current local datetime; used as a consistent helper for timestamps in session objects.
key_constructs.reasoning: Keep a single helper for obtaining timestamps to make it easy to mock/time-control during tests and to localize any timezone or clock adjustments in one place.

key_constructs.name: _hash_id
key_constructs.purpose: Produce a deterministic 12-character hex hash of an identifier string.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to obfuscate PII in prompts while keeping identifiers stable across messages so LLMs can reason about identity without exposing raw IDs.

key_constructs.name: _hash_sender_id
key_constructs.purpose: Return a hashed sender id prefixed with 'user_' using _hash_id.
key_constructs.reasoning: Creates readable but obfuscated sender labels for use in system prompts when redaction is enabled.

key_constructs.name: _hash_chat_id
key_constructs.purpose: Hash the numeric portion of a chat ID while preserving platform prefix if present.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to obfuscate chat identifiers in a way that keeps the platform context; important when injecting home channel IDs or chat references into prompts without revealing raw numeric IDs (phone numbers, group ids).

key_constructs.name: _looks_like_phone
key_constructs.purpose: Heuristic to detect phone-number-like strings for PII handling.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to determine when to treat an identifier as a phone and possibly redact or hash it.

key_constructs.name: SessionSource
key_constructs.purpose: Dataclass describing where a message originated: platform, chat, user, thread, and optional alt IDs and chat topic.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates the origin metadata used for routing responses back to the correct channel, for injecting context into system prompts, and for session key construction. to_dict/from_dict are used to persist/restore routing metadata.

key_constructs.name: SessionContext
key_constructs.purpose: Aggregates SessionSource with connected platforms and configured home channels plus session metadata (IDs and timestamps).
key_constructs.reasoning: Represents the context object used by build_session_context_prompt to create an LLM-facing description of the runtime environment. Holds both the origin and the set of destinations available for scheduled task delivery.

key_constructs.name: build_session_context_prompt
key_constructs.purpose: Construct the dynamic system prompt fragment describing session context (source, connected platforms, home channels, delivery options).
key_constructs.reasoning: This function centralizes how runtime environment info is presented to the LLM. It implements PII redaction when allowed by platform, special-case platform notes (Slack/Discord), multi-user thread behavior, and delivery option formatting which downstream code (skills, cron) relies upon to interpret destination strings returned by the model.

key_constructs.name: SessionEntry
key_constructs.purpose: Dataclass representing an entry in the persistent session index (session_key ↔ session_id mapping and usage metadata).
key_constructs.reasoning: Tracks token accounting, creation/update timestamps, origin metadata for routing, display name, and lifecycle flags (e.g., memory_flushed, was_auto_reset). Provides to_dict/from_dict for persistence to sessions.json and to survive restarts and background expiry flushes.

key_constructs.name: build_session_key
key_constructs.purpose: Deterministically construct the session key string used to identify conversations based on SessionSource and session isolation flags.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function is the authoritative construction for session keys. Its rules (DM vs group/channel vs thread vs per-participant isolation) determine session boundaries, prompt caching behavior, and long-term persistence. Changing its behaviour is a compatibility risk and is validated by tests.

key_behaviors: constructs deterministic session keys for incoming messages
builds dynamic system prompt fragments describing the session environment
redacts or hashes PII for safe LLM exposure depending on platform
models/persists session metadata and lifecycle flags

pitfalls.mistake: Changing build_session_key format or semantics without migrating sessions.
pitfalls.consequence: Existing sessions will be split or merged unexpectedly, breaking prompt caching, transcript retrieval, and routing.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat build_session_key as a compatibility boundary; run session-related tests and check persisted sessions.json and transcript naming when editing.

pitfalls.mistake: Enabling PII redaction for platforms that require raw IDs for mentions (e.g., Discord).
pitfalls.consequence: LLM instructions or responses could fail to tag users correctly or produce unusable mention strings.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect _PII_SAFE_PLATFORMS set; do not change the set without understanding mention semantics and updating dependent routing or message-prefixing logic.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering prompt wording/order in build_session_context_prompt without updating any tests or downstream prompt caches.
pitfalls.consequence: Prompt cache misses and changed model behavior; potential UX regressions for multi-user threads and delivery options.
pitfalls.prevention: Any wording changes must be validated via tests (tests/gateway/test_session.py) and consideration for prompt caching and model expectations.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming thread sessions are per-user when thread_sessions_per_user default is False.
pitfalls.consequence: Incorrect session isolation logic leading to prompt cache thrashing or incorrect user identity presentation.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the thread_sessions_per_user parameter and the comment-history: threads are shared by default unless enabled.

key_sections: SessionSource: canonical source representation and serialization (to_dict/from_dict and description)
build_session_key: single source of truth for session keying and isolation rules
build_session_context_prompt: how runtime context and PII redaction are presented to the LLM
SessionEntry: metadata persisted to sessions.json (token accounting and lifecycle flags)
skip_unless_needed: _now and small hashing helpers (unless modifying timestamps or hashing behavior)
low-level regex constants for phone detection (unless adjusting PII heuristics)

relevant_snippets.description: Tests for SessionSource serialization/description and basic build_session_context_prompt expectations (platform name, chat name).
Tests validating multi-user thread behavior, channel topic presence, Slack/Discord platform notes, and PII handling via prompts.
Tests covering transcript rewrite semantics and corrupt-line skipping for JSONL transcripts.

related_files.relationship: Defines Platform enum, GatewayConfig, PlatformConfig and HomeChannel used throughout this module (SessionSource.platform, build_session_context).
related_files.reason_to_check: When adjusting platform-specific prompt notes, PII policy, or home channel formatting, verify gateway/config.py enum values and HomeChannel structure.

related_files.relationship: Optional SQLite-backed session transcript store that SessionStore may use; load_transcript merges results between JSONL and SQLite.
related_files.reason_to_check: When editing transcript selection logic (prefer longer source), ensure compatibility with DB APIs and expected message ordering.

insights.title: Session key construction changed for thread handling (shared by default)
insights.problem: Previously threads were keyed with participant IDs by default, now threads are shared unless thread_sessions_per_user is enabled.
insights.root_cause: UX change to support shared thread sessions; need to avoid prompt cache thrashing caused by varying user identity injected into system prompt.
insights.solution: Updated build_session_key to accept thread_sessions_per_user flag and avoid appending participant_id when thread_id is present and thread_sessions_per_user is False; updated build_session_context_prompt to show Multi-user thread note instead of single pinned **User:** when shared.
insights.lesson_learned: Session keying policy is a core compatibility boundary; changing defaults requires tests and careful messaging. When sharing a session among participants, prefix messages with sender names so the prompt contains attribution without changing the system prompt.

tests/gateway/test_session.py.short_description: Unit tests for gateway session behavior: session keying, prompt generation, transcript persistence and recovery.
tests/gateway/test_session.py.description:
This test module verifies gateway session semantics and persistence behavior. It exercises SessionSource serialization and description, dynamic session context prompt generation (including platform-specific notes, channel topic inclusion, PII/redaction behavior, and multi-user thread handling), and transcript storage/repair behaviors (JSONL corrupt-line resilience, rewrite semantics, and preference logic when both JSONL and SQLite sources exist). The tests also emulate a SessionStore with or without a DB backend and patch internal loading to isolate filesystem operations.

The test file contains several test classes grouping related behaviors: roundtrip serialization (TestSessionSourceRoundtrip), human-readable description logic (TestSessionSourceDescription), prompt building (TestBuildSessionContextPrompt), JSONL rewrite semantics (TestSessionStoreRewriteTranscript), corrupt transcript handling (TestLoadTranscriptCorruptLines), and logic preferring the longer transcript source between JSONL and SQLite (TestLoadTranscriptPreferLongerSource). Tests mock out SessionStore._ensure_loaded where appropriate and use a temporary filesystem (tmp_path) for creating transcripts and session directories.

key_constructs.name: TestSessionSourceRoundtrip
key_constructs.purpose: Test roundtrip serialization/deserialization for SessionSource and validate default handling and error cases.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures session metadata survives to_dict/from_dict and that platform enums are enforced.

key_constructs.name: TestSessionSourceDescription
key_constructs.purpose: Test the human-readable description property for different chat types and missing fields.
key_constructs.reasoning: Validates description logic used in logs and prompts where friendly strings are shown to operators or LLMs.

key_constructs.name: TestBuildSessionContextPrompt
key_constructs.purpose: Validate build_session_context_prompt output includes platform names, platform-specific notes (Slack/Discord), channel topic and multi-user thread semantics.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures LLM-facing prompts contain the expected guidance and do not include private identifiers when redaction is intended.

key_constructs.name: TestSessionStoreRewriteTranscript
key_constructs.purpose: Regression tests ensuring rewrite_transcript replaces JSONL transcripts on disk and handles empty rewrite.
key_constructs.reasoning: Protects against regressions where /undo or /retry operations would fail to persist truncated history.

key_constructs.name: TestLoadTranscriptCorruptLines
key_constructs.purpose: Regression tests that corrupt JSONL lines are skipped and do not crash transcript loading.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures resilience against mid-write crashes producing truncated JSONL: the loader should skip invalid lines.

key_constructs.name: TestLoadTranscriptPreferLongerSource
key_constructs.purpose: Ensure load_transcript returns whichever source (JSONL or SQLite) has more messages.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents silent truncation when both legacy JSONL history and newer SQLite DB exist for a session.

key_behaviors: validate SessionSource to_dict/from_dict roundtrip
validate build_session_context_prompt contains platform notes and channel topic
ensure transcript rewrite persists truncated history to JSONL
ensure loader skips corrupt JSONL lines and prefers longer source between JSONL and SQLite

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming SessionStore._ensure_loaded will always be called during construction in tests.
pitfalls.consequence: Tests patch this method; constructing SessionStore without the patch may trigger filesystem/DB side effects.
pitfalls.prevention: When constructing SessionStore in tests or fixtures, mirror the patching used here or provide a valid sessions_dir and config.

pitfalls.mistake: Editing prompt text in build_session_context_prompt without updating test assertions that look for exact substrings (e.g., '**Channel Topic:** ...', 'Multi-user thread').
pitfalls.consequence: Test failures indicating changed expectations; prompt cache or LLM behavior could change.
pitfalls.prevention: Run the prompt-related tests after any wording change and update tests intentionally if phrasing must change.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying transcript storage format (JSONL line format) without updating tests that write and parse raw files.
pitfalls.consequence: Loader tests that create truncated/corrupt files will fail or may silently accept different formats, hiding bugs.
pitfalls.prevention: Coordinate any format changes with loader logic and tests that emulate corrupt/truncated files.

key_sections: TestBuildSessionContextPrompt: covers most LLM-facing prompt expectations and edge cases
TestLoadTranscriptCorruptLines: shows how transcripts are written/read from JSONL and how corruption is handled
TestLoadTranscriptPreferLongerSource: important for migration logic between JSONL and SQLite
skip_unless_needed: the many small assertion variants in TestSessionSourceDescription (unless changing description logic)

relevant_snippets.description: Imports and basic SessionSource roundtrip tests demonstrating serialization usage.
Build session context and prompt tests for multiple platforms and channel topic assertions.
Transcript rewrite and corrupt-line tests showing how files are written/read and how JSONL corruption is tolerated.

related_files.relationship: Under-test module implementing session logic and prompt construction.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any edits in gateway/session.py require re-running these tests and may need test updates if semantics or output wording change.

related_files.relationship: Used in tests that simulate an active DB-backed session store (TestLoadTranscriptPreferLongerSource).
related_files.reason_to_check: If the DB API or append_message semantics change, tests that create a SessionDB and append sqlite messages should be updated.

data_constants_to_check: ensure Platform enum in gateway/config.py matches tests' expectations
verify HomeChannel serialization matches build_session_context_prompt consumers

insights.title: Tests added for shared thread session semantics and prompt text
insights.problem: Changing default thread session semantics required tests to ensure prompt construction and key building behave correctly across DM/group/thread cases.
insights.root_cause: Risk of regressions in session keying and prompt caching when changing default behavior.
insights.solution: Added tests for multi-user thread prompt, non-thread group user display, DM thread behavior, and shared vs isolated thread session key construction.
insights.lesson_learned: When altering session semantics, cover DM, group (no thread), group-with-thread, and cross-platform thread shapes in tests.

hermes_cli/auth_commands.py.short_description: CLI subcommands for managing the credential pool (add/list/remove/reset/interactive).
hermes_cli/auth_commands.py.description:
This module implements the hermes CLI credential-pool subcommands and interactive prompts used to add, list, remove, and reset credentials (API keys and OAuth tokens) for model providers. It exists to provide a user-facing wrapper around the agent.credential_pool API and the hermes_cli.auth helpers, translating CLI arguments and interactive input into PooledCredential objects, persisting them via load_pool()/pool.add_entry()/pool.remove_index(), and performing cleanup of seeded credentials (env-backed or singleton OAuth artifacts) so removed credentials don't re-seed on next load.

The module centralizes provider normalization and base URL resolution (including special handling for openrouter and custom-provider config entries). It formats credential metadata for display (including an exhaustion cooldown human-readable formatter) and contains several small helper functions used by both the non-interactive subcommands and the interactive menu flow. It relies heavily on agent.credential_pool types and functions (PooledCredential, AUTH_TYPE_* constants, load_pool, list_custom_pool_providers, etc.) and on hermes_cli.auth.PROVIDER_REGISTRY and other auth helpers for provider-specific OAuth flows.

Design choices evident in the module: the CLI functions keep business logic minimal and delegate persistence/selection to load_pool()/pool APIs; provider normalization accepts multiple aliases and resolves custom provider names from config; the module encodes source prefixes (e.g., manual:, device_code, hermes_pkce, env:) and includes careful teardown steps when removing credentials that were seeded externally (clearing .env entries, removing auth store sections, or deleting specific OAuth files). Several behaviors are unit-tested to ensure non-mutating list semantics (auth_list_command must not call pool.select()).

key_constructs.name: _get_custom_provider_names
key_constructs.purpose: Return list of (display_name, pool_key) tuples from config.custom_providers if present.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to present and resolve custom provider names; changes here affect custom provider resolution and display throughout auth flows.

key_constructs.name: _normalize_provider
key_constructs.purpose: Normalize user-supplied provider identifiers into canonical registry keys (aliases, openrouter, custom pools).
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralized normalization is used by all command entrypoints; any edits must preserve aliasing and custom provider resolution.

key_constructs.name: _provider_base_url
key_constructs.purpose: Return the inference/base URL for a provider, handling openrouter, custom providers, and registry entries.
key_constructs.reasoning: Base URL is written into pooled credentials; changing behavior could alter where requests are routed or how credentials are validated.

key_constructs.name: _format_exhausted_status
key_constructs.purpose: Format an entry's exhaustion/exhausted-until state into a human-readable suffix shown in auth list output.
key_constructs.reasoning: Produces the 'exhausted' cooldown text; tests assert exact substring behavior and time math, so this formatting must remain consistent.

key_constructs.name: auth_add_command
key_constructs.purpose: Non-interactive handler to add credentials for a provider: supports API key and provider-specific OAuth flows.
key_constructs.reasoning: Core add flow creates PooledCredential objects and persists them by calling pool.add_entry(); interacts with provider-specific OAuth helpers and writes provider base_url into the entry.

key_constructs.name: auth_list_command
key_constructs.purpose: Prints all credentials (optionally filtered by provider) and their statuses without mutating pool selection.
key_constructs.reasoning: Reads pool.entries() and pool.peek() only; tests verify it must not call pool.select(), so any edit must avoid selection or mutation.

key_constructs.name: auth_remove_command
key_constructs.purpose: Remove a credential by index/id/label, reindex priorities and perform cleanup of seeded sources (.env, auth store, file-backed OAuth).
key_constructs.reasoning: Per commit history, removal must clear underlying canonical sources for singleton-seeded credentials; code contains explicit branching for env:, device_code, hermes_pkce, and other special sources.

key_constructs.name: _interactive_auth
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level interactive menu that lists pool status and drives add/remove/reset/strategy workflows via prompts.
key_constructs.reasoning: Interactive flow delegates to the same non-interactive command handlers (auth_add_command, auth_remove_command, etc.) so preserving input handling semantics is important.

key_behaviors: creates pooled credentials from CLI input or OAuth flows
lists credentials with human-readable exhausted cooldowns
removes credentials and clears canonical seed sources
resets credential statuses for a provider
provides interactive management workflows

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying auth_list_command to call pool.select() or otherwise mutate pool state while listing.
pitfalls.consequence: Tests (and user expectations) rely on listing being non-mutating; calling select() would change the 'current' credential and could affect runtime selection or break tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Use pool.peek() and pool.entries() only when implementing list behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing how special 'source' strings are interpreted or written (e.g., 'env:', 'manual:hermes_pkce', 'manual:device_code').
pitfalls.consequence: Removal cleanup logic (clearing .env entries, auth store keys, or deleting OAuth files) depends on exact source strings; changing them will prevent correct teardown and may allow re-seeding.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve source prefixes and cleanup branches that check removed.source exact values.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the time math or string formatting in _format_exhausted_status without updating tests.
pitfalls.consequence: Unit tests assert specific substrings and countdown displays (e.g., '59m 30s left'), so formatting changes will break tests.
pitfalls.prevention: If adjusting formatting, run tests that cover exhausted cooldown output and update assertions accordingly.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying provider normalization so custom provider keys are transformed differently.
pitfalls.consequence: Custom providers defined in config may no longer resolve and pool operations could fail to find or create proper pools.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the CUSTOM_POOL_PREFIX normalization logic and _resolve_custom_provider_input fallback.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or changing the code paths that clear singleton-seeded credentials (auth store removal, oauth file unlinking).
pitfalls.consequence: After removal, credentials may be re-seeded on next load and not truly revoked, violating intended behavior and tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the explicit removal steps: remove_env_value for env:, edit auth store under _auth_store_lock for device_code/provider, and remove ~/.anthropic_oauth.json for anthropic hermes_pkce.

key_sections: auth_add_command: central non-interactive entrypoint for adding credentials (creates PooledCredential and writes to pool)
auth_remove_command: remove + cleanup logic for various credential sources (env, device_code, hermes_pkce)
_format_exhausted_status: time math and formatting used by auth_list_command
skip_unless_needed: _interactive_auth and interactive helper prompts (only relevant if editing interactive UX)

relevant_snippets.description: Fixture helpers for writing an auth store and generating JWT-like tokens used across tests.
test_auth_add_api_key_persists_manual_entry — verifies API key add flow writes manual pool entry to auth.json.
OAuth add tests for anthropic/nous/codex verifying provider-specific flows produce pool entries with expected fields.
Tests for removal behavior, reindexing of priorities, and preference of exact label match over numeric index.

related_files.relationship: auth_commands wraps and manipulates CredentialPool and PooledCredential instances provided by this module.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any signature changes in PooledCredential, load_pool, pool.add_entry/remove_index/reset_statuses/resolve_target will require editing auth_commands to match.

related_files.relationship: Provides PROVIDER_REGISTRY, device-code and codex helpers and auth-store utilities used for OAuth flows and clearing tokens.
related_files.reason_to_check: Provider registry or auth-store format changes affect provider handling, label generation, and cleanup logic used in auth_commands.

related_files.relationship: Custom providers and credential_pool_strategies are stored/loaded via config helpers referenced here.
related_files.reason_to_check: Modifying custom provider config shape or env removal helper remove_env_value would affect custom provider resolution and env-clearing logic in remove flow.

insights.title: Clear singleton-seeded credentials from auth store on removal
insights.problem: Removing singleton-sourced credentials (device_code/PKCE) cleared env/static vars but the auth store was re-seeded on next load_pool() call, effectively re-creating the credential.
insights.root_cause: auth_remove_command wasn't removing the underlying auth store entry for singleton sources.
insights.solution: When removing a credential with source == device_code or hermes_pkce, clear corresponding entries from auth store or remove OAuth files (e.g., ~/.anthropic_oauth.json) so the next load_pool() call doesn't re-seed the credential.
insights.lesson_learned: Credential removal must clear all canonical sources (env, auth store, singleton files) to truly revoke access in-app.

tests/test_auth_commands.py.short_description: Unit tests verifying CLI auth subcommands behavior backed by the credential pool and auth store.
tests/test_auth_commands.py.description:
This test module validates the hermes_cli auth subcommands and the integration with the file-backed auth store and credential pool persistence. It creates a temporary HERMES_HOME and writes out an auth.json fixture, then imports and invokes the CLI handlers (auth_add_command, auth_remove_command, auth_reset_command) to assert that the persisted JSON has the expected entries, sources, and fields. The tests also assert non-mutating behavior for listing and string formatting of exhaustion cooldowns. External interactions (OAuth login flows, device-code helpers, credential pool seeding) are monkeypatched to isolate the CLI logic and the auth store file operations.

The tests cover API key addition, several OAuth provider flows (anthropic, nous, openai-codex), removal semantics (reindexing priorities, accepting label targets, preferring numeric labels exactly equal to a credential label over numeric index), reset of exhaustion statuses, and clearing provider-wide auth (clear_provider_auth). They also check that the list command does not mutate pool selection and that exhausted cooldown output uses time math expected by consumers.

key_constructs.name: _write_auth_store
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that writes a JSON auth.json file under a tmp HERMES_HOME for tests to use.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to seed test fixtures; editing tests must preserve semantics of file placement and JSON structure expected by code under test.

key_constructs.name: _jwt_with_email
key_constructs.purpose: Construct a fake JWT-like token whose payload contains an email value used to derive labels from tokens.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by OAuth tests to verify label_from_token behavior; if label derivation changes, these tokens may need changes.

key_constructs.name: test_auth_add_api_key_persists_manual_entry
key_constructs.purpose: Test that adding an API key with auth_add_command writes a 'manual' credential to auth.json.
key_constructs.reasoning: Validates the core add flow for API keys and label handling.

key_constructs.name: test_auth_add_anthropic_oauth_persists_pool_entry
key_constructs.purpose: Test that anthropic OAuth add flow persists a manual:hermes_pkce credential with token/expiry/refresh.
key_constructs.reasoning: Monkeypatches agent.anthropic_adapter.run_hermes_oauth_login_pure to isolate OAuth flow returns.

key_constructs.name: test_auth_remove_reindexes_priorities
key_constructs.purpose: Test that removing an entry reindexes remaining entries' priorities to be contiguous starting at 0.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures remove logic calls pool.remove_index and then writes back priorities; sensitive to pool persistence semantics.

key_behaviors: seed auth store fixtures and verify persistent writes
monkeypatch provider OAuth/device-code helpers to simulate flows
assert non-mutating list output and exhaustion formatting

pitfalls.mistake: Relying on actual environment API keys or real OAuth flows instead of monkeypatching in tests.
pitfalls.consequence: Tests will call external services or pick up dev machine environment secrets causing flakiness or leaking keys in CI.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the autouse fixture that clears env vars and ensure provider helpers are monkeypatched when simulating OAuth/device-code behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the exact formatted strings in _format_exhausted_status without updating tests.
pitfalls.consequence: Assertions that look for substrings like 'exhausted (429)' or '59m 30s left' will fail.
pitfalls.prevention: When updating formatting, update tests to match or adjust assertions to be less brittle.

key_sections: test_auth_add_* functions: show how add flows are invoked and what JSON fields are asserted
tests around removal and listing: demonstrate expectations around reindexing and non-mutating list behavior
skip_unless_needed: Lower-level formatting assertion details unless working on exhaustion formatting

relevant_snippets.description: Helpers and autouse fixture clearing provider env vars for test isolation.
API key add test that inspects auth.json after auth_add_command.
Anthropic/nous/codex OAuth add tests showing how monkeypatching returns are shaped and validated.
Removal and reset tests showing expected reindexing and status clearance.

related_files.relationship: Primary module under test; tests call the module's functions directly to assert auth store writes and printed output.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change in auth_commands function signatures, output text, or persisted JSON format require test updates.

related_files.relationship: Contains provider-specific login helpers and auth store utilities that tests monkeypatch (e.g., _nous_device_code_login, _codex_device_code_login).
related_files.reason_to_check: If those helpers change names or return shapes, tests must update monkeypatches and assertions.

data_constants_to_check: auth.json structure keys: credential_pool, providers, active_provider

hermes_cli/runtime_provider.py.short_description: Resolves the runtime inference provider, base URL, api_mode and credentials for CLI/gateway/cron runtimes.
hermes_cli/runtime_provider.py.description:
This module centralizes selection and resolution of the runtime inference provider and its connection details (provider name, api_mode, base_url, api_key, credential_pool info). It is used by CLI, gateway, cron, and helper entrypoints so a single resolution algorithm drives how the agent chooses and authenticates against model providers. The file implements provider-specific heuristics and precedence rules (explicit overrides > config.yaml model settings > environment > credential pools > auto-detection), and encapsulates special-casing for providers such as Anthropic, OpenAI Codex, OpenRouter, Nous, Copilot, OpenCode variants, and user-defined custom endpoints.

The implementation splits responsibilities across many small helper functions to keep logic testable and composable: URL/api_mode detection, config normalization, custom provider lookup, credential pool selection, explicit override handling, and provider-specific fallbacks. Many behaviors are defensive (strip/None-safe handling of config values, timeouts and exception swallowing for local auto-detection, sentinel 'no-key-required' for unauthenticated custom endpoints). The module integrates with hermes_cli.auth for auth state and agent.credential_pool for pool operations; provider adapters (anthropic, codex, nous) are referenced for token resolution when needed.

Historical fixes and careful semantics appear in the file: auto-detection fallthrough on revoked OAuth tokens (auto vs explicit request difference), not using OAuth access_token as Nous inference credential, warning when custom_providers is a dict, the 'no-key-required' sentinel for local endpoints, and trimming /v1 for OpenCode anthropic compatibility. These semantics are covered by the unit tests in tests/test_runtime_provider_resolution.py and should be preserved or updated together with tests when editing. The explicit vs auto behavior is particularly important: explicit requests surface AuthError from provider token refresh attempts, while auto tries to fall through to other providers when safe.

key_constructs.name: _normalize_custom_provider_name
key_constructs.purpose: Canonicalize a user-supplied custom provider name (lowercase, dash-separated).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to match menu keys and config entries reliably; changes here affect named custom provider lookup.

key_constructs.name: _detect_api_mode_for_url
key_constructs.purpose: Heuristically detect api_mode from a base_url (e.g., api.openai.com -> codex_responses).
key_constructs.reasoning: Minimal heuristic used to choose API mode when config doesn't specify it; altering it changes default api_mode selection.

key_constructs.name: _auto_detect_local_model
key_constructs.purpose: Query a local server's /models endpoint to pick a model id if only one model is loaded.
key_constructs.reasoning: Convenience for local dev; performs a network call with timeout and swallows exceptions. Tests mock behaviors to avoid real network I/O.

key_constructs.name: _get_model_config
key_constructs.purpose: Load and normalize the model section from config.yaml with None-safe string handling and alias support.
key_constructs.reasoning: Single source of truth for model.default and model.base_url used across provider resolution. Many branches rely on its normalized output.

key_constructs.name: _provider_supports_explicit_api_mode
key_constructs.purpose: Decide whether a persisted api_mode in config should be honored for a given runtime provider to avoid leaking modes across providers.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents stale api_mode from a prior provider affecting a new provider; 'custom' is treated specially.

key_constructs.name: _copilot_runtime_api_mode
key_constructs.purpose: Determine Copilot provider api_mode, honoring config when appropriate or delegating to hermes_cli.models.copilot_model_api_mode.
key_constructs.reasoning: Copilot requires provider/model-specific heuristics; fallback to 'chat_completions' on errors is intentional.

key_constructs.name: _parse_api_mode
key_constructs.purpose: Validate and normalize api_mode values from config; return None for invalid inputs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Central validator used to avoid misconfigurations and gate downstream branches.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_runtime_from_pool_entry
key_constructs.purpose: Build a runtime dict from a selected PooledCredential, applying provider defaults and provider-specific rules.
key_constructs.reasoning: Composes final runtime when the credential pool supplies credentials; implements multiple provider heuristics affecting api_mode and base_url.

key_constructs.name: resolve_requested_provider
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve the requested provider name with precedence: explicit arg -> model config -> HERMES_INFERENCE_PROVIDER env -> 'auto'.
key_constructs.reasoning: Controls which provider semantics should be applied; consistent single-entry resolution point for callers.

key_constructs.name: _try_resolve_from_custom_pool
key_constructs.purpose: Attempt to load a credential pool for a custom base_url and return a runtime dict if a usable credential exists.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates pool key logic and selection for custom endpoints; returns None on many failure modes to allow fallthrough.

key_constructs.name: _get_named_custom_provider
key_constructs.purpose: Lookup named custom provider entries from config.yaml (custom_providers list) and validate config shape.
key_constructs.reasoning: Matches user-named providers and logs a warning when custom_providers is malformed (dict instead of list). Tests assert this mapping and warning behavior.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_named_custom_runtime
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve runtime dict for named custom providers, preferring credential pools, then config/api_key, then environment fallbacks.
key_constructs.reasoning: Handles explicit named custom provider flow, including returning 'no-key-required' placeholder for unauthenticated local endpoints when appropriate.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_openrouter_runtime
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve OpenRouter or custom endpoint base_url and api_key selection, honoring config when appropriate and preferring OPENROUTER_API_KEY for OpenRouter URLs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Contains nuanced prioritization of config vs env vs explicit overrides and determines the effective provider label ('openrouter' vs 'custom'). Tests cover many branches here.

key_constructs.name: _resolve_explicit_runtime
key_constructs.purpose: Handle explicit_api_key/explicit_base_url overrides for providers and resolve provider-specific tokens (Anthropic, openai-codex, Nous).
key_constructs.reasoning: Explicit overrides must bypass credential pools and provider-portal refresh flows; this function enforces that, and raises AuthError for missing Anthropic credentials as expected.

key_behaviors: resolve provider and credentials for runtime use
prefer credential pools when available
honor explicit_api_key/explicit_base_url by skipping pools
auto-detect api_mode from base_url or model name
treat named custom providers from config specially

pitfalls.mistake: Treating OAuth 'access_token' as an inference api_key for Nous.
pitfalls.consequence: Sends an incorrect token to inference endpoints (404s or auth errors).
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that Nous requires 'agent_key' for inference and do not fall back to access_token.

pitfalls.mistake: Allowing a persisted api_mode for a different provider to leak into runtime selection.
pitfalls.consequence: Unexpected api_mode leading to API incompatibilities or 400 responses.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain _provider_supports_explicit_api_mode logic that guards application of config api_mode.

pitfalls.mistake: Catching AuthError for explicit provider requests and treating it as auto fallthrough.
pitfalls.consequence: Conceals fatal authentication errors when the user explicitly requested a provider.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve explicit-vs-auto error semantics: auto mode may fall through, explicit must surface AuthError.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing the 'no-key-required' sentinel for unauthenticated custom endpoints.
pitfalls.consequence: Some SDKs will reject an empty api_key string and block use of local/custom no-auth endpoints.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the sentinel placeholder for no-auth cases where the SDK expects a non-empty string.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing OpenCode /v1 trimming rules for Anthropic compatibility.
pitfalls.consequence: Double /v1 in constructed SDK paths can break requests to some providers.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect trimming behavior when api_mode == 'anthropic_messages' and provider is OpenCode.

key_sections: _get_model_config: source of model defaults and config normalization
_try_resolve_from_custom_pool: custom endpoint pool selection logic
_resolve_openrouter_runtime: environment/config/api_key prioritization for OpenRouter and custom endpoints
_resolve_explicit_runtime: explicit override handling and provider-specific error semantics
skip_unless_needed: _auto_detect_local_model: network call used only for local single-model detection
_copilot_runtime_api_mode: copilot-specific heuristics relevant only when provider is copilot

relevant_snippets.description: Credential pool selection test — shows monkeypatching resolve_provider and load_pool and asserts runtime dict fields.
Anthropic pool/config behavior tests — ensure config.base_url is honored and explicit override bypasses pools.

related_files.relationship: Provides resolve_provider, AuthError and auth helpers relied upon by runtime resolution.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to auth state, resolve_provider semantics, or AuthError behavior will affect runtime selection and tests.

related_files.relationship: Defines CredentialPool, PooledCredential, get_custom_provider_pool_key, and load_pool referenced here for pool-based resolution.
related_files.reason_to_check: Modifying pool API or key mapping changes behavior when credential pools exist.

related_files.relationship: Contains provider-specific model api_mode mapping functions referenced by copilot/OpenCode branches.
related_files.reason_to_check: If model api_mode mapping changes, the copilot/OpenCode heuristics must be revisited.

tests_to_run: pytest -q (full unit test suite excluding integration/e2e)

insights.title: Auto-detect should fall through on revoked OAuth tokens
insights.problem: resolve_runtime_provider('auto') could detect an OAuth provider from auth.json and then fail with AuthError during credential refresh, blocking auto-detection even when other env-var providers (e.g., OpenRouter) were available.
insights.root_cause: Code prioritized provider reported by resolve_provider, attempted OAuth refresh (which can raise AuthError) and didn't allow a fallthrough when the user had requested 'auto'. Also, get_nous_auth_status reported logged_in=True if any access_token existed even if expired/revoked.
insights.solution: Wrap resolve_nous_runtime_credentials() and resolve_codex_runtime_credentials() in try/except AuthError; if requested_provider == 'auto' then log and fall through to next provider instead of propagating the error. Keep explicit requested provider behavior to raise the AuthError.
insights.lesson_learned: Auto-detection logic must be resilient to stale auth; only surface auth refresh failures as fatal when the user explicitly requested that OAuth provider. Tests should cover auto-fallthrough vs explicit request behavior.

insights.title: Don't use OAuth access_token as Nous inference credential
insights.problem: Fallback to access_token (OAuth bearer token for Nous portal API) was being used as an inference API key when agent_key was missing, causing 404s from inference endpoints.
insights.root_cause: Code used state.get('agent_key') or state.get('access_token') as the api_key for runtime provider resolution.
insights.solution: Remove access_token fallback — only use agent_key for inference; an empty agent_key allows resolve_nous_runtime_credentials() to mint a fresh key.
insights.lesson_learned: Different tokens (portal OAuth vs inference) are not interchangeable — treat credential types explicitly and avoid permissive fallbacks.

insights.title: Warn when custom_providers is a dict (YAML list vs dict mistake)
insights.problem: If user wrote custom_providers as a dict, the runtime provider resolver returned None silently, making debugging hard.
insights.root_cause: Malformed YAML shape for custom_providers was not diagnosed; caller simply treated non-list as no providers.
insights.solution: Log a warning when custom_providers is a dict suggesting run 'hermes doctor' and clarifying that list items must be prefixed with '-'.
insights.lesson_learned: Detect common mistaken YAML shapes and log actionable warnings rather than failing silently.

insights.title: Use placeholder API key for custom providers that require no auth
insights.problem: Local/custom OpenAI-compatible runtimes that don't require authentication were rejected by SDKs when api_key was empty.
insights.root_cause: An empty string api_key was passed through to provider configs; some SDKs treat empty string as an attempt to authenticate and reject it.
insights.solution: Set api_key to the sentinel placeholder 'no-key-required' when no real key is present, matching behavior used elsewhere.
